proposals.title: Assessing the Representativeness of Station ALOHA for Basin-Scale Biogeochemical Variability in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre
proposals.motivation: The North Pacific Subtropical Gyre (NPSG) is a critical region for carbon sequestration and nitrogen fixation, with Station ALOHA serving as its principal long-term observatory. The foundational assumption that this single station captures basin-scale natural and anthropogenic change underpins major syntheses and climate feedback projections. However, emerging pre-2026 work on microbial genome size selection and diazotroph distribution suggests high spatial heterogeneity in microbial community function, challenging the validity of extrapolating from a single point to an entire biome.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the biogeochemical signals and microbial community functional traits (e.g., nitrogen fixation potential, carbon export efficiency) measured at Station ALOHA are not representative of the spatial variability across the NPSG, particularly for processes mediated by microbial symbioses and diazotrophs.
proposals.method: We will conduct a meridional and zonal transect survey across the NPSG, replicating core HOT program measurements. Microbial genomic DNA will be sequenced to assess functional gene abundance (e.g., nifH for nitrogen fixation) and estimate genome sizes as a trait. We will employ geostatistical analysis (kriging) and multivariate statistical tests (PERMANOVA) to quantify the variance explained by distance from Station ALOHA. A bootstrap resampling algorithm will determine the number and location of stations required to estimate basin-scale means within a defined confidence interval.
proposals.contribution: This work will rigorously test a foundational assumption in ocean biogeochemistry, providing a quantitative framework for scaling point measurements to basin and global scales. It will directly inform the design of future observing networks and improve the parameterization of microbial-mediated processes (N2 fixation, carbon cycling) in global ocean models.
proposals.novelty: While spatial variability is acknowledged, this study uniquely integrates microbial genomic trait data (genome size, functional genes) with traditional biogeochemical metrics to explicitly test the representativeness of a legendary time-series site, moving beyond simple chemical inventories to the underlying microbial drivers.

proposals.title: Integrating Microbial Genome Size, Diazotroph Dynamics, and Phytoplankton Resilience into a Mechanistic Model for Forecasting Ocean Carbon and Nitrogen Cycling Under Climate Change
proposals.motivation: The ocean's biological carbon pump and nitrogen cycle are critical regulators of climate, yet their responses to global change remain poorly constrained. The Hawaii Ocean Time-series (HOT) provides a unique, multi-decadal record of ocean biogeochemistry, but its predictive power for future states is limited without models that incorporate key microbial and ecological controls. This work addresses the urgent need to forecast how microbial community traits, like genome streamlining and diazotroph activity, will modulate ecosystem resilience and biogeochemical feedbacks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating empirical constraints on microbial genome size, nitrogen fixation rates, and phytoplankton resilience into a mechanistic model will significantly improve the skill of forecasts for ocean carbon export and nitrogen inventory under future climate scenarios.
proposals.method: We will leverage the HOT dataset (physical, chemical, biological) to develop and train a hybrid model. A process-based ocean biogeochemistry model (e.g., a modified version of the Biogeochemical Elemental Cycling model) will be coupled with machine learning emulators. The ML components, using gradient-boosted regression trees or neural networks, will parameterize key relationships—such as those between environmental conditions, microbial genome size (as a proxy for metabolic strategy), and diazotroph activity—informed by Karl's prior work. The integrated model will be forced with IPCC CMIP6 future climate projections to generate 50-year forecasts.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, empirically constrained forecasting framework for ocean biogeochemistry. It will deliver specific projections for changes in carbon export and nitrogen fixation in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre, a critical oligotrophic biome. The model will serve as a testbed for evaluating how microbial adaptation and selection influence climate-relevant ecosystem functions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of microbial genomic trait data (genome size) and globally derived diazotroph distributions as predictive model parameters, moving beyond traditional bulk nutrient parameterizations to forecast ocean biogeochemical cycles.

proposals.title: Revealing cryptic phenology in marine microbial communities: A time-domain analysis of multi-decadal ocean time-series data
proposals.motivation: The phenology of marine microbes, including diazotrophs and phytoplankton, governs critical biogeochemical cycles like nitrogen fixation and carbon export. However, traditional analyses of ocean time-series data often fail to detect complex, non-stationary periodicities in biological activity, limiting our ability to predict ecosystem responses to climate change. This gap hinders our understanding of microbial resilience and its feedbacks on global biogeochemistry, as highlighted in recent work on the North Pacific and global databases.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying advanced time-domain signal processing techniques from astronomy will reveal previously cryptic, multi-scale periodic cycles in microbial community activity and nitrogen fixation rates that are not captured by conventional statistical methods. These cycles are linked to physical and chemical drivers and are predictive of ecosystem state transitions.
proposals.method: We will apply the Lomb-Scargle periodogram and wavelet analysis (specifically using the Morlet wavelet) to high-frequency, multi-decadal datasets from the Hawaii Ocean Time-series (HOT) and other global stations. These algorithms are designed for unevenly sampled time-series data and can identify non-stationary periodic signals. We will analyze time-series of key variables: chlorophyll fluorescence (proxy for phytoplankton), nifH gene abundances (for diazotrophs), nitrate concentrations, and particulate organic carbon. The resulting periodicities will be cross-correlated with physical drivers (e.g., sea surface temperature, mixed layer depth) to identify forcing mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for defining the phenological 'fingerprint' of marine microbial ecosystems, directly linking cyclic biological activity to biogeochemical rates. It will generate testable predictions about the timing and magnitude of nitrogen fixation and carbon cycling events, enhancing the predictive power of models like those used in the Global Oceanic Diazotroph Database. The analysis will offer new metrics for assessing microbial community resilience to environmental perturbation.
proposals.novelty: While time-series analysis is common in oceanography, the direct application of sophisticated astronomy-derived algorithms (Lomb-Scargle, wavelet transforms) to dissect biological cycles in microbial community data is novel. This approach moves beyond linear trends and simple annual harmonics to uncover complex, interacting periodicities that may govern ecosystem function.

proposals.title: Low Functional Redundancy in Deep-Sea Microbial Communities Constrains Biogeochemical Resilience
proposals.motivation: Deep-sea microbial communities mediate globally significant carbon and nitrogen fluxes, yet their functional resilience is poorly understood. The prevailing paradigm assumes high functional redundancy buffers these ecosystems against perturbation. Testing this assumption is critical for predicting how deep-ocean biogeochemistry, a major carbon sink, will respond to climate-induced changes in organic matter flux and oxygenation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deep-sea microbial communities exhibit low functional redundancy for key carbon and nitrogen cycling processes, making deep-ocean biogeochemical functions more vulnerable to environmental change and species loss than currently assumed.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Station ALOHA time-series and global oceanographic datasets to analyze metagenomic and metatranscriptomic sequences from the mesopelagic and bathypelagic zones. Using tools like METABOLIC and HUMAnN, we will quantify the taxonomic breadth of genes and pathways for critical processes (e.g., nitrification, denitrification, organic matter remineralization). Network analysis and null modeling will be employed to statistically assess redundancy by comparing observed co-occurrence patterns of functional genes against randomized communities, controlling for environmental gradients.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first basin-scale, gene-centric assessment of functional redundancy in the deep ocean, directly challenging a key ecological assumption. It will establish a quantitative framework linking microbial community structure to biogeochemical resilience, informing global models of ocean carbon and nitrogen cycling under climate change scenarios.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the long-held, but largely untested, assumption of high deep-sea functional redundancy by applying modern genomic tools to a global biogeochemical problem. It moves beyond cataloging genomic potential to statistically test redundancy, connecting microbial ecology directly to ecosystem-scale functional vulnerability.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Particle-Attached Microbial Contribution to Bulk 'Planktonic' Community Structure and Function in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre
proposals.motivation: Standard oceanographic surveys characterize microbial communities via filtration of bulk seawater, operationally defined as 'planktonic.' This potentially conflates truly free-living cells with those attached to unsampled, sinking, or suspended particles, which host distinct, metabolically active communities. Given the critical roles of microbes in ocean carbon sequestration and nitrogen fixation, misattributing the source of metabolic signals could bias our understanding of elemental cycling and ecosystem resilience in oligotrophic systems like the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant fraction of the genomic and functional potential recovered from standard bulk seawater filtrates in the oligotrophic ocean originates from particle-attached microbes, not the free-living fraction, thereby skewing our perception of community structure and biogeochemical rates.
proposals.method: We will conduct paired sampling during HOT (Hawaii Ocean Time-series) cruises, comparing standard size-fractionated filtration (e.g., 0.2-3.0 µm for 'free-living') with a novel, gentle tangential flow filtration and density gradient separation protocol to physically isolate truly free-living cells from particle-associated ones. Community structure will be assessed via 16S/18S rRNA gene amplicon and metagenomic sequencing. We will apply source-tracking algorithms (e.g., SourceTracker2) to the bulk metagenomic data, using the isolated free-living and particle-attached metagenomes as source end-members, to quantify the particle-attached contribution. Functional analysis will focus on nitrogen fixation (nifH) and carbon metabolism genes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first quantitative estimate of the particle-attached 'contamination' in standard planktonic surveys, refining the interpretation of global marine microbiome datasets. It will directly test the assumption that bulk water sampling reflects free-living processes, with implications for models of carbon export and the revised estimates of global N2 fixation from databases like GODDv2.
proposals.novelty: While particle-attached communities are studied separately, no study has systematically quantified their contribution to the standard 'planktonic' metagenome in the oligotrophic ocean, a gap critical for interpreting global biogeochemical models and microbial genome size evolution studies that rely on bulk sampling.

proposals.title: Linking Depth-Resolved Microbial Metabolic Gene Abundance to In Situ Nitrogen and Carbon Cycling Rates in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in marine metagenomics, a persistent gap exists between quantifying the genetic potential of microbial communities and measuring actual biogeochemical process rates in situ. This disconnect limits our ability to predict how ocean ecosystems, particularly oligotrophic gyres like the North Pacific, will respond to climate change. Bridging this gap is essential for moving from descriptive genomic inventories to mechanistic, predictive models of elemental cycling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that depth-stratified abundances of key metabolic genes (e.g., for nitrogen fixation, nitrification, ammonium oxidation, and carbon fixation) will show strong, predictable correlations with directly measured in situ process rates, and that these relationships will vary systematically across the euphotic, mesopelagic, and bathypelagic zones.
proposals.method: We will leverage time-series samples and concurrent rate measurements from the Station ALOHA HOT program. Depth-resolved microbial DNA will be sequenced (shotgun metagenomics) and processed using the METABOLIC pipeline (v4.0) to quantify gene abundances for targeted N and C pathways. These genomic profiles will be statistically correlated with a multi-year suite of coincident in situ rate measurements (e.g., 15N2-based N2 fixation, 13C-based primary production, 15N-based nitrification). Relationships will be modeled using multivariate analyses (e.g., Random Forest regression) to identify the most predictive genomic features for each process.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a quantitative framework linking genomic potential to realized function in a key oceanic biome, directly testing assumptions underlying 'omics-based biogeochemical models. It will refine global estimates of N2 fixation and carbon export by providing mechanistic, gene-based constraints on process distributions with depth. The resulting dataset will be a benchmark for validating and improving ecosystem and Earth system models.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies catalog genes or measure rates independently, this proposal's novelty lies in the systematic, depth-resolved pairing of comprehensive metabolic potential data with a long-term, co-located suite of direct rate measurements from a well-characterized oceanographic time-series site, enabling a direct test of gene abundance as a predictor of in situ biogeochemical activity.

proposals.title: Decoupling Temperature and Nutrient Constraints: A Global Assessment of Diazotroph Biogeography and Activity in Cold, Nutrient-Rich Marine Systems
proposals.motivation: The prevailing paradigm in marine biogeochemistry, reinforced by global databases like GODv2, holds that biological N2 fixation is largely confined to warm, oligotrophic waters. This assumption directly influences global nitrogen and carbon cycle projections under climate change. However, recent discoveries of nifH genes in cold, high-latitude waters and genomic evidence of abiotic selection pressures on marine microbes suggest this constraint may be an oversimplification, potentially leading to significant underestimates of oceanic N2 fixation and its feedbacks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that diverse, cold-adapted and/or nutrient-tolerant diazotrophic symbioses and free-living microbes are active contributors to marine N2 fixation, and that their activity is governed by a combination of iron availability, microbial community interactions, and energetic constraints rather than by temperature and phosphate limitation alone.
proposals.method: We will integrate a targeted field campaign in the North Pacific subpolar gyre and Gulf of Alaska with a global meta-analysis. Fieldwork will employ high-sensitivity 15N2 tracer assays coupled with metagenomic and metatranscriptomic sequencing of size-fractionated samples to quantify rates and identify active diazotrophs, including potential symbiotic associations with eukaryotic phytoplankton. Concurrently, we will apply machine learning algorithms (e.g., Random Forest regression) to the expanded GODv2 database and global oceanographic reanalysis data (e.g., temperature, nutrients, trace metals) to identify environmental predictors of diazotroph occurrence and to map potential habitats beyond traditional warm oligotrophic zones.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, mechanism-based challenge to the canonical temperature-nutrient paradigm for oceanic N2 fixation. It will deliver a revised, physiologically-informed habitat model for global diazotrophs and produce new, direct rate measurements from understudied cold systems, thereby refining global N and C budget estimates. The findings will directly inform models of phytoplankton resilience and biogeochemical feedbacks in a changing climate.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond correlative observations to mechanistically test the environmental constraints on diazotrophy by targeting cold, nutrient-rich systems explicitly excluded by the current paradigm and by integrating modern genomic tools with rate measurements to identify active, novel nitrogen-fixing consortia.

proposals.title: Expansion and Quantification of UCYN-A Nitrogen Fixation in the Pan-Arctic Marine System
proposals.motivation: The Arctic Ocean is undergoing rapid climate-driven changes, including sea ice loss and increased stratification, which may alter nutrient regimes and favor nitrogen-fixing organisms. Pre-2026 studies have documented the presence of the unicellular cyanobacterial symbiont UCYN-A in the Bering and Chukchi Seas, but its activity and biogeochemical significance across the broader Arctic remain unquantified. This gap limits our ability to predict how new nitrogen inputs may moderate carbon export and ecosystem resilience in these climatically sensitive, oligotrophic waters.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that UCYN-A symbioses are active nitrogen fixers across multiple Arctic seas (e.g., Beaufort, East Siberian, Laptev, Kara), and that their N2 fixation rates are governed by a combination of physical (stratification, light) and biogeochemical (iron, phosphate) factors distinct from lower-latitude regimes.
proposals.method: We will conduct ship-based surveys in target Arctic seas, collecting discrete water samples for rate measurements and molecular analyses. N2 fixation rates will be quantified using the 15N2 tracer assimilation method with careful attention to tracer dissolution. Concurrently, we will measure UCYN-A abundance via quantitative PCR (targeting *nifH* genes) and assess active transcription. Environmental parameters (macronutrients, dissolved iron, temperature, salinity, PAR) will be characterized. Statistical modeling (multiple linear regression and generalized additive models) will be used to identify the key drivers of UCYN-A distribution and activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first pan-Arctic rate measurements for UCYN-A, directly contributing to the Global Oceanic Diazotroph Database. It will constrain the regional and potential future contribution of biological N2 fixation to Arctic nitrogen budgets. The findings will improve parameterization of nitrogen cycling in Arctic biogeochemical models, informing projections of net community production and carbon sequestration under climate change.
proposals.novelty: While UCYN-A has been detected in the Arctic, its functional role as a nitrogen source is presumed but not systematically measured beyond limited regions. This proposal moves from presence to process, testing the non-obvious premise that these symbioses are a quantitatively significant nitrogen source across diverse, cold, and seasonally ice-covered Arctic ecosystems.

proposals.title: Projecting the Climate Sensitivity of High-Latitude Nitrogen Fixation: Integrating the UCYN-A Symbiosis into Arctic Ocean Biogeochemical Models
proposals.motivation: The Arctic Ocean is experiencing rapid warming, sea ice loss, and increased stratification, which may expand niches for diazotrophs. Pre-2026 studies, including the Global Oceanic Diazotroph Database, show evidence of N2 fixation in polar waters, but current Earth System Models (ESMs) largely ignore high-latitude diazotrophy. This omission creates a critical uncertainty in projecting future marine nitrogen and carbon cycles, as Arctic N2 fixation could become a significant new nitrogen source, potentially altering regional productivity and carbon export.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating the physiological and ecological constraints of the high-latitude UCYN-A symbiosis into biogeochemical models will reveal a significant, climate-sensitive increase in Arctic N2 fixation by 2100, with measurable feedbacks on net primary production and carbon sequestration.
proposals.method: We will develop a new mechanistic module for the UCYN-A symbiosis within the Community Earth System Model (CESM) ocean biogeochemical component. The module will parameterize UCYN-A growth and N2 fixation based on pre-2026 field data (temperature, light, nutrients, host availability) from the North Pacific and Arctic. We will implement a trait-based algorithm linking symbiont genome size (informed by Karl's work on abiotic selection) to thermal adaptation and nutrient stress resilience. Future projections (RCP 8.5) will be run to quantify changes in N2 fixation rates and their biogeochemical impacts.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first process-based, climate-projection-ready model of high-latitude N2 fixation, directly addressing a key uncertainty in the marine nitrogen cycle. It will deliver quantitative estimates of future Arctic nitrogen input and its potential to moderate or amplify climate-driven changes in productivity, thereby refining projections of ocean carbon uptake.
proposals.novelty: While global models include tropical diazotrophs, this work uniquely focuses on integrating a polar-adapted symbiotic system (UCYN-A) whose activity and climate response are poorly constrained. The explicit linkage of microbial genomic traits (e.g., genome size) to ecosystem function for high-latitude N2 fixers within a global ESM framework is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Environmental Determinants of Nitrogen Fixation by the Polar UCYN-A Symbiosis: A Multi-Omic and Biogeochemical Assessment
proposals.motivation: The discovery of active nitrogen (N2) fixation in cold, high-latitude waters challenges the long-held paradigm that diazotrophy is restricted to warm, oligotrophic oceans. The unicellular cyanobacterial symbiont UCYN-A is a key player in these polar systems, but the specific environmental and physiological factors enabling its activity in cold, nutrient-rich waters remain poorly understood. Resolving these controls is critical for accurately quantifying global oceanic N2 fixation, modeling nitrogen and carbon cycles under climate change, and predicting ecosystem resilience in rapidly warming polar regions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that polar UCYN-A activity is enabled by a unique combination of factors: (1) a symbiotic partnership that provides a stable, high-energy microenvironment, (2) access to elevated dissolved iron from coastal/shelf sources or glacial melt, and (3) specific thermal and light niches within the summer stratified surface layer.
proposals.method: We will conduct targeted field campaigns in the North Pacific subpolar gyre and the Arctic Ocean, regions with documented UCYN-A presence. We will measure in situ N2 fixation rates (15N2 tracer) alongside a comprehensive suite of physicochemical parameters (T, nutrients, Fe speciation, PAR). Activity will be correlated with UCYN-A genotype abundance (qPCR) and transcriptional activity (metatranscriptomics of *nifH* and key metabolic pathways). We will employ a machine learning framework (Random Forest regression) to identify the dominant predictors of UCYN-A activity from our multivariate dataset and contextualize our findings within the Global Oceanic Diazotroph Database version 2 (GODDv2).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic, process-oriented model for polar N2 fixation by UCYN-A, directly informing global biogeochemical models. It will refine estimates of high-latitude nitrogen input, with implications for understanding carbon export potential. Furthermore, it will elucidate the fundamental physiological limits and symbiosis dynamics of a key marine microbe expanding its niche.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have reported the *presence* of UCYN-A in cold waters, this proposal uniquely integrates rate measurements, trace metal biogeochemistry, and symbiosis-focused molecular ecology to identify the *causal drivers* of its activity, moving beyond correlation to mechanism.

proposals.title: Seasonal Dynamics and Biogeochemical Impact of the UCYN-A Symbiosis in the Changing Arctic Ocean
proposals.motivation: The Arctic Ocean is experiencing rapid climate-driven changes, including sea ice loss and increased stratification, which are predicted to alter nutrient cycling. The uncultivated, symbiotic cyanobacterium UCYN-A is a globally significant nitrogen fixer, but its activity and ecological role in polar regions remain poorly characterized. Understanding its seasonal population dynamics is critical for predicting future nitrogen and carbon fluxes in this vulnerable, warming ecosystem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that UCYN-A populations in the Arctic exhibit distinct seasonal abundance peaks and metabolic activity linked to sea ice retreat and phytoplankton bloom phases, and that their N2 fixation provides a significant source of new nitrogen that supports primary production in the post-ice melt period.
proposals.method: We will conduct a high-resolution, pan-Arctic survey across seasonal and spatial gradients of ice cover. UCYN-A abundance (host and symbiont) will be quantified via qPCR targeting *nifH* and host 18S rRNA genes. Metabolic activity will be assessed via RT-qPCR of *nifH* transcripts and nanoscale secondary ion mass spectrometry (nanoSIMS) on sorted cells. Environmental context (nutrients, chlorophyll, POC/PON) will be measured. Data will be integrated using multivariate statistical models and correlated with satellite-derived sea ice and ocean color data to identify key environmental drivers.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, seasonal quantification of UCYN-A population dynamics and N2 fixation activity in the Arctic. It will directly constrain the contribution of this symbiosis to the Arctic nitrogen budget and test its role in ecosystem resilience to climate change. The results will refine global biogeochemical models by incorporating a previously underappreciated polar nitrogen source.
proposals.novelty: While UCYN-A has been studied in temperate and tropical oceans, its seasonal ecology in the rapidly changing, ice-influenced Arctic is virtually unknown. This work uniquely integrates symbiosis-specific molecular activity measurements with pan-Arctic biogeochemical observations to resolve its functional role in a climatically critical region.

proposals.title: Eddy-Mediated Biogeographic Shifts: Integrating Mesoscale Nitrate Flux and Larval Connectivity to Model Terebellid Worm Range Dynamics in the North Pacific
proposals.motivation: Climate change is altering mesoscale eddy fields, which are critical for lateral nutrient transport, particularly of nitrate, fueling productivity in oligotrophic gyres. The ranges of benthic organisms like terebellid polychaetes, which rely on surface-derived organic matter, are likely governed by these dynamic eddy-driven resource subsidies and larval dispersal pathways, yet current species distribution models (SDMs) fail to resolve these sub-basin scale physical-biological couplings. This gap limits our ability to predict biogeographic shifts and identify potential climate refugia in the vast open ocean.
proposals.hypothesis: The future biogeography of terebellid polychaetes in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre will be determined by climate-driven changes in mesoscale eddy activity, which concurrently modulate larval connectivity and create mosaics of benthic habitat quality through the lateral advection of isotopically distinct nitrate pools that alter food web baselines.
proposals.method: We will couple a high-resolution (1/10°), eddy-resolving regional ocean model (ROMS) configured for the North Pacific with a biogeochemical module (CoSiNE) to simulate present and future (RCP 8.5) 3D nitrate fields and eddy kinetic energy. Simulated eddy pathways and nitrate flux will drive two integrated models: 1) an individual-based model (IBM) for larval dispersal using Ichthyop, and 2) a MaxEnt SDM where habitat suitability is informed by eddy-mediated nitrate flux signatures (derived from δ¹⁵N isoscapes) and seafloor particulate organic carbon flux. The coupled model will project range shifts by 2100.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel mechanistic framework linking mesoscale physics to benthic biogeography, moving beyond correlative SDMs. It will generate the first high-resolution maps of potential 'eddy refugia' and 'eddy stress corridors' for deep-sea fauna in the North Pacific, offering testable predictions for biogeographic change. The methodology will be transferable to other marine systems where lateral advection is a key ecological driver.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of eddy-resolving nitrate flux, isotopic food web tracers, and larval connectivity within a single biogeographic model is unprecedented. It posits that the *same* physical feature (an eddy) directly affects both the demographic (larval supply) and niche-based (adult habitat quality) determinants of species distributions, a synergy ignored in prior studies.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Keystone Role of Betapista dekkerae in Benthic-Pelagic Coupling: An Experimental Test of Trophic Impact and Biogeochemical Function
proposals.motivation: Understanding the functional roles of specific organisms in marine ecosystems is critical for predicting biogeochemical feedbacks to climate change. The tubeworm Betapista dekkerae is a large, suspension-feeding polychaete that may act as a keystone species in oligotrophic benthic habitats like the North Pacific, potentially influencing carbon and nitrogen cycling through its feeding and physical structure. However, its precise trophic position, dietary sources, and causal impact on sediment stability and nutrient fluxes remain unquantified, limiting our ability to model benthic-pelagic coupling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Betapista dekkerae functions as a keystone species by (1) occupying a distinct trophic position that links pelagic and benthic food webs, and (2) exerting a disproportionate, causal influence on sediment biogeochemistry and community structure through its feeding and biogenic habitat modification.
proposals.method: We will conduct a manipulative field experiment in the oligotrophic North Pacific, comparing control plots with plots where B. dekkerae is physically excluded. We will use stable isotope analysis (δ13C, δ15N) of worm tissue and potential food sources to quantify trophic position and dietary contributions via Bayesian mixing models (e.g., MixSIAR). eDNA metabarcoding (targeting 18S rRNA and 16S rRNA genes) of gut contents and ambient sediment will characterize prey spectra and associated microbiomes. Biogeochemical fluxes (O2, NH4+, NO3-) and sediment erodibility will be measured in situ in exclusion and control plots to quantify functional impact.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first experimental, causal evidence for the keystone role of B. dekkerae, directly linking organismal biology to ecosystem function. It will advance the field by quantifying a specific mechanism of benthic-pelagic coupling in an oligotrophic ocean and will deliver novel data on the trophic ecology and microbiome of a poorly studied ecosystem engineer, informing global models of marine carbon and nitrogen cycles.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Karl and others has mapped global diazotroph distributions and considered phytoplankton resilience, this study uniquely applies a rigorous, multi-tool experimental framework (exclusion, SIA, eDNA, flux measurements) to test the causal role of a single metazoan species in benthic nutrient cycling—a scale of organismal focus often missing from large-scale biogeochemical surveys.

proposals.title: Network Analysis of a Novel Benthic Polychaete: Inferring the Ecological Role of Betapista dekkerae via Co-occurrence Patterns in the North Pacific
proposals.motivation: The recent discovery of the polychaete genus Betapista, including B. dekkerae, in oligotrophic ocean habitats presents a classic ecological challenge: determining the function of a new species with unknown direct interactions. In marine microbial ecology, network analysis of co-occurrence patterns has proven powerful for generating hypotheses about the ecological roles of uncultivated microorganisms. Applying this established statistical framework to a newly described macrofaunal species represents a novel synthesis to accelerate understanding of its place in benthic community structure and biogeochemical cycles.
proposals.hypothesis: The co-occurrence network position of Betapista dekkerae across environmental gradients will reveal statistically significant associations with specific macrofauna, indicating its potential role as a keystone species, a competitor, or a resource specialist within North Pacific benthic ecosystems.
proposals.method: We will conduct systematic, replicated benthic surveys using ROV or submersible transects at known and suspected B. dekkerae habitats in the North Pacific. High-resolution imagery and physical sampling will quantify the abundance and distribution of B. dekkerae and all co-occurring observable macro-organisms. Pairwise species associations will be calculated using the SparCC algorithm, designed to infer robust correlations from compositional count data. The resulting network will be constructed and analyzed using the igraph package in R to identify modules, centrality metrics (e.g., betweenness centrality), and key positive/negative links involving B. dekkerae.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative ecological profile for the novel genus Betapista, generating specific, testable hypotheses about its biotic interactions and functional role. It will demonstrate the transfer of a core microbial ecology method to macrofaunal ecology, providing a template for investigating other newly discovered species. The results will guide targeted future studies on the feeding biology and direct impacts of B. dekkerae on carbon and nitrogen cycling in oligotrophic sediments.
proposals.novelty: While co-occurrence network analysis is standard for microbes, its deliberate application to generate initial ecological hypotheses for a newly described marine macrofaunal species is a novel methodological crossover. This approach bypasses the initial, often lengthy period of direct behavioral observation to rapidly map a new species into its community context.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic Extent of Microbial Membrane Lipid Remodeling in the Ocean: A Test Beyond Alphaproteobacteria and Flavobacteria
proposals.motivation: Marine microbial membrane lipid composition, particularly the substitution of phospholipids with non-phosphorus lipids under phosphate stress, is a critical biochemical adaptation with implications for global ocean carbon and phosphorus cycling. Current knowledge is largely confined to model Alphaproteobacteria (e.g., SAR11) and Flavobacteria, leaving a major gap in understanding its prevalence across the broader microbial community that drives ocean biogeochemistry. Determining the phylogenetic breadth of this response is essential for accurately modeling microbial resilience and elemental feedbacks in changing oligotrophic ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the conserved lipid substitution response is a phylogenetically widespread adaptation among dominant marine bacterial phyla beyond the currently studied groups, and that its genomic and biochemical signatures will correlate with environmental phosphate gradients in the North Pacific and other oligotrophic gyres.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and newly generated metagenomic and metatranscriptomic datasets from the Hawaii Ocean Time-series (HOT) and other global ocean surveys (e.g., Tara Oceans) to screen for lipid remodeling genes (e.g., sqd, bta, plsX, pgp) across diverse bacterial phyla, including Gammaproteobacteria, Bacteroidota (other than Flavobacteria), Planctomycetota, and Verrucomicrobiota. Lipidomic analyses will be performed on size-fractionated particulate organic matter and bacterial cell concentrates from nutrient-amendment incubation experiments in the North Pacific to biochemically validate lipid substitutions. Phylogenetic distribution will be mapped against environmental parameters using multivariate statistical models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the phylogenetic map of a key microbial survival strategy, directly informing models of marine phosphorus cycling and carbon export. It will reveal whether lipid remodeling is a universal oligotrophic adaptation or a trait of specific lineages, thereby refining our understanding of microbial community structure and function in the context of global change.
proposals.novelty: While lipid remodeling is known in isolated groups, a systematic phylogenetic assessment across the uncultivated majority of marine bacteria has not been conducted. This study moves beyond cataloging genome size or diazotroph diversity to probe a core biochemical mechanism linking community genomics to ecosystem-scale biogeochemistry.

proposals.title: Quantifying Phosphorus Stress-Induced Carbon Flux into Sulfolipid and Betaine Lipid Pathways in North Pacific Subtropical Gyre Phytoplankton Using 13C Isotopic Tracer Experiments
proposals.motivation: Phosphorus (P) stress is a key regulator of primary production and carbon cycling in oligotrophic oceans like the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre (NPSG). Pre-2026 work shows microbes adapt to P-limitation by synthesizing non-phosphorus membrane lipids (e.g., sulfolipids, betaine lipids), but the quantitative carbon flux into these alternative pathways and its impact on cellular carbon partitioning and biogeochemical feedbacks remains unknown. This gap limits our ability to predict how marine ecosystems and the biological carbon pump will respond to changing nutrient regimes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that under phosphorus stress, dominant phytoplankton in the NPSG will re-route a significant fraction of fixed carbon into the biosynthesis of sulfolipids and betaine lipids, and that this metabolic shift can be quantified and linked to changes in cellular elemental stoichiometry and growth efficiency.
proposals.method: We will conduct shipboard incubation experiments in the NPSG using 13C-bicarbonate tracer additions under ambient and phosphorus-amended conditions. Particulate organic matter will be collected over time series. Lipids will be extracted and separated via liquid chromatography. The 13C enrichment in specific lipid classes (phospholipids, sulfolipids, betaine lipids, and neutral storage lipids) will be quantified using coupled gas chromatography and isotope ratio mass spectrometry (GC-IRMS). Metabolic flux analysis will be performed by modeling the 13C incorporation kinetics into each lipid pool to calculate absolute carbon flux rates, integrating these with parallel measurements of bulk carbon fixation, cellular P quotas, and phytoplankton community composition.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative rates of carbon diversion into P-free lipid pathways in a natural phytoplankton assemblage, directly testing theories of metabolic adaptation to nutrient stress. It will deliver a mechanistic framework linking nutrient physiology to carbon allocation, refining models of ocean ecosystem resilience and biogeochemical cycling under climate change.
proposals.novelty: While lipid remodeling is known, applying precise 13C metabolic flux analysis to quantify carbon partitioning among specific alternative lipid pathways in a natural oligotrophic community is a novel integration of biochemical and biogeochemical approaches, moving beyond genomic predictions to direct rate measurements.

proposals.title: Forensic Apportionment of Low-δ¹⁵N Signals in the North Pacific: Testing the Role of Local Nitrogen Recycling vs. Remote Fixation
proposals.motivation: Low δ¹⁵N values in marine sediments and particulate matter are routinely interpreted as evidence for remote nitrogen fixation, a key source in global ocean models. This assumption, however, overlooks the potential for in situ processes like dissimilatory nitrate reduction to ammonium (DNRA) or intense ammonification to generate an identical isotopic signal. Resolving this ambiguity is critical for accurately quantifying the ocean's fixed nitrogen budget and its sensitivity to climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of the low-δ¹⁵N signal in oligotrophic systems like the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre is generated locally via sedimentary or water column nitrogen recycling, not solely imported via diazotrophy. This can be tested by identifying a geochemical tracer uniquely associated with local recycling processes.
proposals.method: We will analyze a suite of process-specific tracers in sediment cores and sinking particles from the North Pacific. The primary local process tracer will be the δ¹⁵N of individual amino acids, specifically the elevated ¹⁵N-depletion in glutamate during microbial resynthesis via the intracellular 'Schiff base' reaction, a known signature of degradation and recycling. This will be coupled with bulk δ¹⁵N, organic carbon and nitrogen content, and redox-sensitive trace metals (e.g., Mo, U). We will apply a multi-tracer Bayesian mixing model (e.g., MixSIAR) to statistically apportion the low-δ¹⁵N signal between a putative 'remote fixation' end-member (characterized by high Fe:P, cyanobacterial hopanoids) and a 'local recycling' end-member.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, tracer-constrained test of a fundamental assumption in marine nitrogen cycle studies. It will yield a more mechanistic framework for interpreting paleo-δ¹⁵N records and refine estimates of the spatial distribution and magnitude of N₂ fixation versus recycling, directly informing global models of oceanic nutrient limitation and feedbacks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting forensic source apportionment techniques to a core biogeochemical problem and using amino acid δ¹⁵N—a tracer of intracellular metabolic processing—as a direct fingerprint for local nitrogen recycling, moving beyond correlation-based inferences.

proposals.title: Direct Measurement of In Situ Nitrogen Recycling Timescales in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre via a Synchronized SF6 Dye and ¹⁵N-Nitrate Tracer Release Experiment
proposals.motivation: Persistent subsurface low-δ¹⁵N signals in oligotrophic gyres, like the North Pacific, suggest a decoupling of nitrogen cycling from physical transport, potentially driven by intense local microbial recycling. However, pre-2026 studies lack direct, rate-based evidence to distinguish whether this isotopic signature is generated in situ or imported. Quantifying this timescale is critical for accurately modeling the ocean's nitrogen inventory and its feedbacks to climate change, as local recycling efficiency directly impacts carbon export and ecosystem resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: The development of a low-δ¹⁵N signature in particulate and dissolved nitrogen pools within a defined water parcel occurs on a timescale shorter than the physical dilution and export of that parcel, providing definitive evidence that the signal is generated by rapid in situ recycling.
proposals.method: We will conduct a dual-tracer release experiment in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre at the site of a documented subsurface low-δ¹⁵N signal. A conservative tracer, sulfur hexafluoride (SF6), will be released to tag the water parcel and quantify its physical dispersion rate via high-resolution shipboard measurements. Concurrently, a pulse of ¹⁵N-labeled nitrate will be injected into the same SF6-tagged patch. We will track the isotopic evolution (δ¹⁵N) of particulate organic nitrogen (PON), dissolved organic nitrogen (DON), and ammonium over time, using isotope ratio mass spectrometry. The rate of ¹⁵N tracer dilution into the low-δ¹⁵N background will be compared directly to the rate of SF6 dilution to calculate the net recycling timescale.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, experimentally derived timescale for the generation of the enigmatic low-δ¹⁵N signal, a major outstanding question in marine biogeochemistry. It will quantitatively constrain the relative roles of local microbial recycling versus physical transport in shaping oceanic nitrogen isotope distributions, thereby improving the parameterization of nitrogen cycling in global biogeochemical models.
proposals.novelty: While dual-tracer studies have been used for physical transport and nutrient uptake separately, the synchronized application of a conservative dye and an isotopic nutrient tracer to directly compete biological versus physical timescales for a specific isotopic signature is a novel experimental design. This approach moves beyond correlation to establish causation in the source of the low-δ¹⁵N signal.

proposals.title: Direct Isotopic Tracing of the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre's Low-δ¹⁵N Nitrate Core to Western Pacific Diazotroph Source Waters
proposals.motivation: The subsurface nitrate pool of the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre (NPSG) is characterized by a remarkably low δ¹⁵N signature, widely interpreted as an integrated signal of regional N₂ fixation. However, the physical pathways connecting this isotopically distinct nitrate core to its presumed source regions of intense diazotrophy in the western Pacific remain unresolved. Directly linking the subsurface nitrate anomaly to its source is critical for quantifying the basin-scale nitrogen budget, understanding the coupling of N and C cycles, and predicting how these biogeochemical feedbacks may respond to climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: The low-δ¹⁵N nitrate core within the NPSG is directly advected from the western tropical Pacific, and its spatial evolution in concentration and isotopic composition can be used to trace its origin to specific diazotroph bloom regions and quantify associated N₂ fixation fluxes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a high-resolution meridional transect along ~152°W, coupled with a targeted zonal section from the dateline to the western Pacific warm pool, using the R/V Kilo Moana. Continuous subsurface sampling will analyze nitrate δ¹⁵N and δ¹⁸O, dissolved organic nitrogen (DON) concentration and δ¹⁵N, and nifH gene abundance. We will employ a Lagrangian particle-tracking model, seeded in known western Pacific diazotroph hotspots, forced by high-resolution ocean state estimates (e.g., ECCO2). The model will simulate the advection and mixing of the low-δ¹⁵N signal, and its output will be statistically compared to the observed isotopic fields using an optimal interpolation scheme to identify the most probable source regions and constrain physical transport timescales.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, water-mass-based linkage between western Pacific N₂ fixation and the central Pacific nitrate pool, moving beyond correlation to mechanistic understanding. It will yield a spatially explicit estimate of N₂ fixation export, constrain the isotopic imprint of marine diazotrophy, and refine global models of oceanic nitrogen cycling by providing ground-truthed transport parameters for fixed nitrogen.
proposals.novelty: While the low-δ¹⁵N core is a known feature, this study is novel in its combined use of targeted isotopic transects and Lagrangian modeling to perform a *direct* water-mass tracing exercise, explicitly testing the physical connectivity between source and sink, rather than inferring it from basin-scale budgets or remote sensing.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Dynamic Habitat of a Low-δ¹⁵N Biogeochemical Anomaly in the North Pacific Using Ecological Niche Models
proposals.motivation: The low-δ¹⁵N core in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre (NPSG) represents a significant, yet poorly constrained, source of isotopically light nitrogen to the oligotrophic ocean, challenging our understanding of N2 fixation's spatial and temporal dynamics. Pre-2026 work, including the Global Oceanic Diazotroph Database, has mapped N2 fixers, but the biogeochemical manifestation of their integrated activity—the low-δ¹⁵N signal—has not been modeled as an ecological entity. Understanding its habitat is crucial for predicting how this key nitrogen source responds to climate variability.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatiotemporal distribution of the low-δ¹⁵N core is predictable using species distribution frameworks, with its niche defined by a specific, seasonally variable combination of physical (e.g., sea surface temperature, mixed layer depth) and biogeochemical (e.g., phosphate, N:P ratio) parameters.
proposals.method: We will apply Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM) to multi-year, seasonal δ¹⁵N-PON data from the ALOHA time-series and other NPSG transects. Presence/absence of the low-δ¹⁵N core will be modeled against concurrent satellite-derived and in situ environmental layers using the MaxEnt algorithm. Model calibration and evaluation will employ spatial block cross-validation to ensure robustness, and future projections will be made under pre-2026 CMIP5 climate scenarios to assess sensitivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first predictive, mechanistic map of a key isotopic biogeochemical feature, directly linking microbial process rates to their ecosystem-scale signature. It will identify the primary environmental controllers on the low-δ¹⁵N core's emergence, refining estimates of N2 fixation's spatial footprint and its sensitivity to ocean warming and stratification.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in treating a biogeochemical isotopic ratio as a modelable 'species,' fusing time-series biogeochemistry with ENM techniques common in macroecology but rarely applied to such microbial metabolic signatures at the ocean basin scale.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Transformational History of Export Flux: A δ¹⁵N Provenance Framework for Nitrogen Cycling in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre
proposals.motivation: The biological pump's efficiency is governed by the suite of nitrogen transformations between dissolved nitrate assimilation and particle export, but these integrated processes remain a poorly quantified 'black box'. This limits our ability to predict how marine nitrogen cycling and carbon export will respond to climate-driven changes in oligotrophic ecosystems, such as the expansion of subtropical gyres.
proposals.hypothesis: The isotopic offset (Δδ¹⁵N) between subsurface nitrate and sinking particulate organic nitrogen (PON) at multiple depths can be deconvolved to quantify the relative contributions of key processes—including phytoplankton assimilation, zooplankton grazing, microbial remineralization, and particle aggregation—that govern the transformational history of exported nitrogen.
proposals.method: We will conduct high-resolution, depth-stratified sampling in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre (Station ALOHA) to measure δ¹⁵N-NO₃⁻ and δ¹⁵N of size-fractionated sinking particles from sediment traps. The core analytical framework adapts isotopic mixing models (e.g., Bayesian Stable Isotope Mixing Models, SIMMs) used in sedimentary provenance studies. We will treat the subsurface nitrate δ¹⁵N as the source signature and model the sinking particle δ¹⁵N as a mixture altered by process-specific isotopic fractionation factors (ε) for assimilation, remineralization, and grazing, constrained by prior experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond simple isotopic comparisons to provide a novel, process-constrained metric for the integrated nitrogen transformation network in the mesopelagic. It will directly test hypotheses about microbial and zooplankton controls on export efficiency, offering a mechanistic framework to incorporate into global biogeochemical models for improved prediction of climate-biogeochemistry feedbacks.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in explicitly applying the conceptual and quantitative framework of sedimentary provenance tracer studies to the water column nitrogen cycle, transforming δ¹⁵N from a source indicator into a diagnostic tracer of the integrated transformation pathway.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Isotopic Imprint of N2 Fixation and Nitrate Assimilation on Export Flux in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre
proposals.motivation: The biological pump's efficiency is governed by the sources and transformations of nitrogen supporting export. In oligotrophic gyres like the North Pacific, low-nitrate waters are sustained by N2 fixation and recycled nitrogen, but the relative contribution of these sources to sinking particulate organic nitrogen (PON) remains poorly quantified. Directly comparing the δ¹⁵N of subsurface nitrate with the δ¹⁵N of sinking particles collected by sediment traps provides a critical, integrated tracer to resolve these pathways and their climate sensitivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the δ¹⁵N of sinking PON in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre will be significantly lower than the δ¹⁵N of subsurface nitrate, reflecting a dominant isotopic signature of N2 fixation exported from the surface ocean, and that this offset will vary spatially and seasonally with physical forcing and microbial community structure.
proposals.method: We will pair high-precision δ¹⁵N measurements of nitrate (using the denitrifier method) from depth profiles with δ¹⁵N measurements of sinking PON from time-series sediment traps deployed at the Station ALOHA and at contrasting sites along a gradient of diazotroph influence. Samples will be analyzed via elemental analyzer-isotope ratio mass spectrometry. The isotopic mass balance will be modeled using a Bayesian mixing model (e.g., MixSIAR) to partition the nitrate and N2-fixation contributions to export, integrating these results with concurrent genomic and biogeochemical data from the Simons Collaboration on Ocean Processes and Ecology (SCOPE) and the Hawaii Ocean Time-series (HOT).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first coupled nitrate-PON δ¹⁵N assessment to directly constrain the export flux attributable to N2 fixation versus nitrate assimilation at Station ALOHA. It will yield a novel quantitative framework for evaluating nitrogen source partitioning in export models and inform predictions of how the expansion of oligotrophic gyres may alter the isotopic composition and magnitude of the biological pump.
proposals.novelty: While δ¹⁵N of nitrate and particles have been studied separately, simultaneous, depth-resolved measurement of both pools in a sustained time-series to directly trace the isotopic transformation into export flux is novel. This approach moves beyond source identification to quantify the isotopic imprint of microbial processes (diazotrophy, nitrification, assimilation) on the exported nitrogen sink.

proposals.title: Quantitative Source Tracking of Diazotroph Communities in the Soil-Sediment-Compaction Continuum Using nifH Gene Variant Signatures
proposals.motivation: Understanding the provenance and transport of nitrogen-fixing microbes (diazotrophs) within terrestrial and coastal systems is critical for predicting ecosystem-scale nitrogen inputs and biogeochemical feedbacks. Current methods, such as those in the Global Oceanic Diazotroph Database, map distributions but lack the quantitative framework to trace specific populations from source to sink. This limits our ability to model how anthropogenic practices, like soil amendment with compost, alter microbial-mediated nitrogen cycling.
proposals.hypothesis: Distinct compartments of the soil-sediment-compaction continuum (SSCC) harbor unique, resident diazotroph communities whose genetic signatures (nifH variants and associated genomic markers) can serve as quantitative tracers to model their dispersal and intermixing across the pathway.
proposals.method: We will adapt Microbial Source Tracking (MST) frameworks by treating SSCC compartments (e.g., source soil, compost feedstock, finished compost, amended soil) as distinct microbial reservoirs. Using high-throughput sequencing of the nifH gene and metagenomic data from these compartments, we will identify compartment-specific genetic markers through source-library-based algorithms like the Bayesian Monte Carlo Markov Chain (BMCMC) method used in fecal source tracking. These markers will be validated and applied in a mixing model to quantify the proportional contributions of different source communities to diazotroph assemblages in downstream environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, source-attribution model for diazotroph dispersal in terrestrial systems, directly linking management practices to microbial community outcomes. It will advance microbial biogeography by introducing a proven hydrological tracing framework to soil and marine sediment ecology, offering a new tool to assess the resilience and connectivity of nitrogen-fixing communities under environmental change.
proposals.novelty: The application of a formal, quantitative MST framework—common in water quality studies—to trace functional microbial guilds across environmental continua is novel. It moves beyond descriptive community mapping to a predictive model of microbial provenance, a concept not yet applied to diazotrophs in the SSCC context.

proposals.title: Linking Host Morphology and Microbial Function: A Geometric Morphometric and Molecular Analysis of Diazotroph-Associated Structures in Marine Invertebrates
proposals.motivation: Marine nitrogen fixation, a critical process for global ocean productivity, is increasingly recognized to occur in symbiotic associations, such as within the chaetae of terebellid worms. However, the mechanistic links between the physical structure of the host habitat and the identity, function, and evolutionary selection of the resident diazotrophs remain poorly understood. Quantifying this form-function relationship is essential for predicting how these symbioses, and thus oceanic nitrogen inputs, may respond to environmental change.
proposals.hypothesis: The three-dimensional morphology of host structures (e.g., polychaete chaetae) acts as a selective filter, shaping the phylogenetic composition and nif gene abundance of associated diazotroph communities, and that specific morphological features correlate with microbial functional potential.
proposals.method: We will apply 3D geometric morphometrics using micro-CT scanning to quantify shape variation in diazotroph-hosting structures (e.g., chaetae from Pacific *Terebella* spp.). Landmark and semi-landmark data will be analyzed via Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Procrustes ANOVA. Concurrently, DNA from micro-dissected structures will be used for 16S rRNA and *nifH* gene amplicon sequencing, and metagenomic sequencing for a subset. Statistical integration will employ methods like Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) and Mantel tests to correlate shape principal components with microbial community composition and *nif* gene abundance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative framework linking host morphology to microbial function in marine diazotrophic symbioses. It will advance the field from parallel characterization to mechanistic understanding, providing novel insights into how host evolution shapes microbial partnerships. The findings will refine models of symbiotic nitrogen fixation's role in ocean biogeochemistry.
proposals.novelty: The application of high-resolution geometric morphometrics to microbial symbiosis ecology is novel, moving beyond descriptive morphology to statistically test form-function linkages. This approach directly integrates organismal biology with molecular microbial ecology in a way not previously attempted for marine diazotrophs.

proposals.title: Lagrangian Attribution of Low-δ¹⁵N Signatures in the North Pacific: Quantifying the Role of N₂ Fixation and Remineralization Pathways
proposals.motivation: The nitrogen isotopic composition (δ¹⁵N) of nitrate is a key tracer for ocean biogeochemistry, with low values in subtropical gyres often attributed to N₂ fixation. However, disentangling the contributions of local fixation from the lateral advection of low-δ¹⁵N waters and heterotrophic remineralization remains a major challenge, limiting our ability to quantify the basin-scale impact of diazotrophs on carbon export. This ambiguity hinders accurate predictions of oligotrophic ecosystem feedbacks to climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that backward-in-time Lagrangian particle tracking, dynamically coupled to a biogeochemical model simulating δ¹⁵N evolution, will reveal that observed low-δ¹⁵N signatures in the central North Pacific are primarily generated by a combination of local N₂ fixation and the advection of waters whose isotopic signal was set by N₂ fixation upstream, with minimal alteration by subsurface remineralization processes.
proposals.method: We will embed virtual particles in observed low-δ¹⁵N waters using the Connectivity Modeling System (CMS) driven by high-resolution (e.g., LLC4320) MITgcm output for the Pacific. Each particle will carry tracers for nitrate, phosphate, N*, and δ¹⁵N, updated along trajectories by a coupled, simplified biogeochemical module simulating N₂ fixation (with a δ¹⁵N ~ -1‰), water column denitrification, and remineralization. The model will use a probabilistic framework to attribute isotopic changes at each time step to specific processes, creating a process-weighted origin map.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, process-attributed origin map for nitrogen in the North Pacific subtropical gyre, directly testing linkages between physical circulation and biogeochemical signatures. It will deliver a novel metric for the 'influence domain' of marine N₂ fixation, refining global estimates and offering a new framework to assess microbial resilience and biogeochemical feedbacks in oligotrophic seas.
proposals.novelty: The integration of Lagrangian tracking with *dynamic*, process-resolving δ¹⁵N tracers moves beyond passive trajectory analysis to a causal, diagnostic tool, explicitly partitioning the observed isotopic signal into contributions from distinct N-cycle processes along advective pathways.

proposals.title: Quantifying Physical Transport Controls on Marine Nitrogen Isotope Homogenization: A Dye-Tracer Calibrated Lagrangian Modeling Study in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre
proposals.motivation: The δ¹⁵N of nitrate is a critical integrator of oceanic nitrogen cycling, reflecting processes like N₂ fixation, denitrification, and assimilation. However, interpreting its distribution is confounded by the unknown timescales and pathways of physical transport that mix distinct isotopic signals. Pre-2026, models and observations operate in parallel, lacking a direct empirical link to constrain the physical dispersion that governs isotope homogenization across ocean basins.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatial variance in nitrate δ¹⁵N in the oligotrophic North Pacific is primarily governed by physical mixing timescales, which can be accurately quantified by Lagrangian particle models calibrated with in situ dye tracer dispersion data.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled release of Rhodamine WT dye in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre, tracking its three-dimensional dispersion over weeks with autonomous vehicles and shipboard sensors. Concurrently, we will run high-resolution (eddy-permitting or eddy-resolving) Lagrangian particle tracking simulations (e.g., using the Parcels framework) initialized in the same location and time. The observed dye dispersion rates and timescales will be used to iteratively calibrate the numerical model's sub-grid parameterizations. The calibrated model will then be used to trace back the origins of water parcels sampled for nitrate δ¹⁵N, calculating physical transport times and pathways to source regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first directly calibrated, mechanistic estimate of physical transport timescales controlling nitrate δ¹⁵N distributions. It will quantitatively partition the roles of physical mixing versus local biological transformation in setting isotopic signatures, thereby refining global oceanic N₂ fixation estimates and their coupling to carbon export. The calibrated model framework will be a new tool for diagnosing biogeochemical tracer transport.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of a deliberate dye release experiment—a proven method in physical oceanography—with state-of-the-art Lagrangian biogeochemical modeling to solve a long-standing isotopic interpretation problem, creating an empirical 'ground truth' for tracer transport that has not been achieved before.

proposals.title: Quantifying Eddy-Driven Nitrate Isotope Exchange: A Laboratory Turbulence Analogy to Parameterize Mesoscale Ocean Mixing
proposals.motivation: Mesoscale eddies dominate lateral nutrient transport in oligotrophic ocean gyres, critically influencing nitrogen availability and primary production. Current coarse-resolution biogeochemical models lack robust, mechanistic parameterizations for this sub-grid-scale eddy-driven exchange, creating uncertainty in projections of marine ecosystem resilience and global nitrogen cycling under climate change. A controlled, physics-first approach is needed to isolate the fundamental turbulent mixing processes.
proposals.hypothesis: The lateral exchange of nitrate between water masses by mesoscale eddies can be described by a universal scaling relationship based on dimensionless numbers (Rossby, Reynolds), and this exchange imparts a quantifiable isotopic (δ¹⁵N) signature that serves as an integrative tracer of the mixing process.
proposals.method: We will adapt chemical engineering reactor analysis by constructing a laboratory-scale rotating tank to simulate a two-layer ocean with reproducible, quasi-geostrophic eddy fields. Two water masses, one enriched in ¹⁵N-nitrate and one with natural abundance, will be introduced. Using Particle Image Velocimetry and high-precision isotope-ratio mass spectrometry, we will quantify the lateral flux and isotopic evolution of nitrate as a function of systematically varied eddy parameters (rotation rate, density contrast). The data will be used to develop and test a parameterization for eddy-induced nitrate exchange based on non-dimensional numbers.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first directly measured parameterization for mesoscale eddy-driven nitrate exchange, explicitly including isotopic tracers. It will provide a physics-based, empirically grounded module to improve the representation of lateral nutrient supply in global ocean models like those used in the Earth System Model community, thereby reducing uncertainty in projections of oceanic nitrogen fixation and carbon export.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in applying controlled physical analogies from engineering turbulence research to an open-ocean biogeochemical problem, and in directly coupling hydrodynamic measurements with dual nitrate isotope tracers to constrain mixing processes mechanistically.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical Bayesian Synthesis of Global Oceanic Nitrate δ¹⁵N: Constraining the Marine Nitrogen Cycle from Regional Data to a Global Mass Balance
proposals.motivation: The stable nitrogen isotope ratio (δ¹⁵N) of oceanic nitrate is a powerful integrator of N cycle processes, including N2 fixation, denitrification, and assimilation, but a quantitative, uncertainty-aware global budget remains elusive. Pre-2026 efforts, including Karl's work in the North Pacific, have produced rich but regionally heterogeneous datasets that are difficult to reconcile into a single framework. A robust global δ¹⁵N budget is critical for testing hypotheses about climate-driven changes in N sources and sinks and their feedbacks on carbon sequestration.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical Bayesian data assimilation framework, analogous to those used for global carbon budgets, can successfully integrate disparate regional nitrate δ¹⁵N measurements and process knowledge to produce a probabilistically consistent global isotope mass balance. We hypothesize this synthesis will reveal systematic biases in current regional flux estimates and identify key data gaps.
proposals.method: We will adapt the hierarchical Bayesian inversion architectures used in atmospheric CO2 flux modeling (e.g., TransCom) to the oceanic N isotope system. The model will treat the global ocean as interconnected boxes, with prior distributions for regional δ¹⁵N fluxes and isotopic signatures informed by Karl's Pacific data and other regional databases (e.g., GEOTRACES). A Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampler (e.g., Stan or PyMC) will be used to assimilate observational data, constrained by the global mass balance of ¹⁴N and ¹⁵N as the top-level hierarchical prior, and propagate uncertainties through the network.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first formally probabilistic global budget of oceanic nitrate δ¹⁵N, quantifying uncertainties for major N fluxes. It will directly test the consistency of elevated global N2 fixation estimates with isotopic data. The open framework will serve as a foundational tool for integrating future data and assessing climate change impacts on marine N cycling.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct methodological analogy, applying a mature carbon-cycle data assimilation framework to the structurally similar but isotopically distinct problem of global nitrogen isotope budgeting—a synthesis not yet attempted pre-2026. It moves beyond regional compilations to a statistically rigorous, process-constrained global inverse model.

proposals.title: Inverse Estimation of Denitrification Rates in the North Pacific Using Morphometric Signatures of Biogenic Minerals
proposals.motivation: Quantifying the rates and spatial patterns of microbial nitrogen loss processes like denitrification is critical for understanding the ocean's nitrogen inventory and its coupling to carbon cycling, but remains poorly constrained by bulk geochemical measurements. This gap limits our ability to model past and future oceanic biogeochemical feedbacks to climate change. Biogenic minerals, such as authigenic barite, form in the water column and preserve isotopic signatures (e.g., δ¹⁵N) of the local nitrogen cycle, but their morphology may encode additional, untapped information about formation dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: The high-dimensional shape of biogenic mineral particles is a quantifiable, multivariate signature that covaries with the kinetic regime of microbial nitrogen transformations during formation, allowing inverse estimation of process rates from combined morphometric and isotopic data.
proposals.method: We will apply geometric morphometrics (e.g., elliptical Fourier analysis and Procrustes superimposition) to quantify the 2D/3D shape of biogenic barite particles from sediment cores in the North Pacific. These shape descriptors will be fused with particle-specific δ¹⁵N measurements into a Bayesian inverse model. The model will treat shape and isotope data as joint outputs of a reaction-diffusion system simulating denitrification kinetics, using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) to inversely estimate the most likely rate parameters and their spatial gradients.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, spatially resolved paleo-proxy for microbial nitrogen cycle rates, directly linking mineral morphology to process kinetics. It will provide new, independent constraints on the magnitude and variability of denitrification in oligotrophic gyres, refining global oceanic nitrogen budgets and their sensitivity to climate perturbations.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of geometric morphometrics—a tool from systematic biology—with isotope-enabled inverse biogeochemical modeling to decode process rates from mineral shape is a fundamentally new approach. It moves beyond using minerals as passive isotopic archives to treating them as dynamic recorders of environmental kinetics.

proposals.title: Evolutionary drivers of sediment biogeochemistry: Phylogenetic prediction of nitrogen-phosphorus coupling in Terebellid polychaete bioturbation
proposals.motivation: Marine sediment bioturbation is a critical ecosystem function, mediating benthic-pelagic coupling of carbon and nutrients like nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P). However, the functional diversity of bioturbators is poorly understood, and we lack a predictive framework for how evolutionary history shapes their biogeochemical impact. Integrating evolutionary biology with isotope biogeochemistry can reveal whether phylogenetic divergence underlies distinct nutrient cycling pathways, addressing a key gap in understanding ecosystem function in a changing ocean.
proposals.hypothesis: Phylogenetic divergence within the Terebellidae family predicts distinct isotopic signatures of nitrate (δ¹⁵N) and phosphate (δ¹⁸O) in burrow microenvironments, indicating that evolutionary innovations in behavior and physiology drive specific modes of N-P coupling during bioturbation.
proposals.method: We will construct a robust molecular phylogeny of Terebellidae using targeted sequencing of mitochondrial (COI) and nuclear (18S, 28S) markers, analyzed with maximum likelihood (RAxML) and Bayesian (MrBayes) algorithms. For key monophyletic clades identified, we will conduct shipboard mesocosm experiments with individuals from the North Pacific, sampling burrow water for concurrent measurement of δ¹⁵N-NO₃⁻ and δ¹⁸O-PO₄³⁻ via isotope ratio mass spectrometry. Statistical comparison (e.g., phylogenetic ANOVA) will test for clade-specific isotopic signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, mechanistic link between polychaete evolutionary history and sediment biogeochemical function, moving beyond descriptive correlation. It will provide a novel phylogenetic framework for predicting functional diversity in bioturbation, directly contributing to models of benthic nutrient cycling and feedbacks in marine ecosystems under climate change.
proposals.novelty: While isotope tracing and phylogenetics are established, their fusion to use evolutionary history as a predictive framework for specific N-P coupling pathways in bioturbation is novel. This approach tests an evolutionary mechanism for functional biogeochemical diversity, a significant advance over prior studies that treat bioturbators as a uniform functional group.

proposals.title: Testing the Stability of Marine Diazotroph Niche Space Under Simulated Climate Forcing in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre
proposals.motivation: Climate change is rapidly altering ocean temperature, stratification, and nutrient regimes, with uncertain consequences for keystone nitrogen-fixing (diazotrophic) microbes. The stability of their fundamental niche—the full range of environmental conditions where they can persist—is a critical unknown for predicting future marine nitrogen and carbon cycling. This gap hinders our ability to forecast ecosystem resilience and biogeochemical feedbacks, as highlighted by recent work on phytoplankton resilience and global N2 fixation estimates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fundamental niche of key, globally distributed marine diazotroph lineages (e.g., *Crocosphaera*, *Trichodesmium*, UCYN-A) is not static but will contract or shift under simulated multi-stressor climate change scenarios, particularly in the oligotrophic North Pacific Subtropical Gyre (NPSG).
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-year perturbation experiment at Station ALOHA using continuous culture chemostats (Ocean Microbial Observatories) to simulate future ocean conditions (elevated pCO2, temperature, altered N:P ratios). Using high-frequency, flow-cytometry coupled cell sorting and subsequent single-cell genome sequencing, we will track lineage-specific population dynamics and genomic adaptations (e.g., gene expression, genome size variation). Niche boundaries will be quantified using pre-2026 ecological niche modeling (MaxEnt algorithm) trained on in-situ environmental data and global diazotroph database (version 2) observations, then tested against experimental outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental test of fundamental niche stability for marine diazotrophs under combined climate stressors, directly linking physiological response to biogeographic theory. It will deliver a refined, mechanism-based parameterization for models predicting future oceanic N2 fixation and carbon export, addressing a key uncertainty in the global nitrogen cycle.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has mapped diazotroph distributions, this proposal uniquely employs controlled, in-situ experimental manipulation of the NPSG microbiome to empirically challenge niche stability, moving beyond correlative observations to causal understanding.

proposals.title: Assessing the Biophysical Feedback of Marine Diazotroph Blooms on Upper Ocean Thermal and Optical Properties in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre
proposals.motivation: Marine biogeochemical models traditionally treat the physical environment (e.g., temperature, light) as a one-way driver of phytoplankton dynamics. However, dense surface blooms of buoyant, pigment-rich cyanobacteria like Trichodesmium and Richelia could modify their local physical milieu by altering light absorption and surface albedo, potentially creating a biophysical feedback loop. This feedback, if significant, could influence local stratification, nutrient dynamics, and carbon export, with implications for projecting ecosystem responses to climate change in oligotrophic systems like the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre (NPSG).
