proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-abundance patches of diazotrophic cyanobacteria can measurably modify the in-water light field and sea surface temperature (SST) through increased pigment-based light absorption and the formation of surface scums, creating a localized, biologically mediated microclimate that feeds back on growth rates and nitrogen fixation.
proposals.method: We will integrate in situ data from Station ALOHA time-series cruises (measuring diazotroph abundance, pigments, and hyperspectral optics) with satellite-derived SST and ocean color (MODIS, VIIRS). A coupled 1-D biophysical model will be developed, building on frameworks like the NOAA EcoLight/DISORT radiative transfer code, to simulate light penetration and heating profiles with and without observed bloom biomass. Statistical causal inference techniques, specifically convergent cross-mapping (CCM) from empirical dynamic modeling, will be applied to the high-resolution time-series to test for potential causality between cyanobacterial abundance and perturbations in SST/light attenuation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first quantitative assessment of a potential biophysical feedback loop in the open ocean, moving beyond correlation to test for causality. It will deliver a novel, empirically constrained parameterization of how phytoplankton biomass can modify its physical environment, which can be integrated into next-generation Earth System Models to improve projections of oceanic nitrogen cycling and carbon export under climate change.
proposals.novelty: While the abiotic control of microbes is well-studied, explicitly testing for an *inverted* causal link where microbial abundance modifies core physical drivers (temperature, light) at ecologically relevant scales in the pelagic ocean is a paradigm-challenging and largely unexplored avenue of research.

proposals.title: Interactive effects of ocean stratification, mixed layer dynamics, and acidification on marine nitrogen fixation and carbon export: A trait-based modeling approach
proposals.motivation: Future ocean biogeochemistry will be shaped by the simultaneous, non-linear effects of multiple climate stressors, including warming-induced stratification, shoaling mixed layers, and ocean acidification. Current Earth System Models (ESMs) often treat these drivers independently or with coarse parameterizations, limiting our ability to project changes in key processes like nitrogen fixation, which fuels marine productivity and carbon sequestration. A mechanistic, integrated assessment is needed to resolve feedbacks between changing physical habitats and microbial ecosystem function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the interactive effects of enhanced stratification, variable mixed layer depth, and reduced pH will non-additively alter the biogeography and rate of oceanic nitrogen fixation, with cascading effects on phytoplankton community structure and the biological carbon pump.
proposals.method: We will develop a regional implementation of a trait-based ecosystem model (e.g., DARWIN, FlexPFT) within a high-resolution physical-biogeochemical framework for the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre, a well-studied diazotroph habitat. The model will explicitly simulate key microbial functional groups, including diazotrophs (Trichodesmium, UCYN-A), and incorporate mechanistic, strain-specific responses of N2 fixation and growth to interactive changes in light (via MLD), temperature, nutrient supply (via stratification), and pH. Model parameterization will be constrained by the Global Oceanic Diazotroph Database version 2 and prior empirical relationships. Simulations will force the model with CMIP6 multi-stressor future scenarios to isolate and quantify interactive effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, mechanistically detailed projection of how concurrent climate drivers interact to control marine nitrogen and carbon cycling. It will directly inform the next generation of global ESMs by providing improved parameterizations for diazotroph function and microbial resilience under multi-stressor conditions. The results will offer a more robust estimate of future oceanic nutrient supply and carbon export potential.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have examined single drivers, this model uniquely integrates the simultaneous and potentially synergistic effects of stratification, light regime, and acidification on the ecophysiology of key nitrogen-fixing organisms, moving beyond phenomenological relationships to a trait-based, mechanistic framework.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Relative Contribution of Bacteria and Archaea to the Production of Biorefractory Dissolved Organic Matter in the North Pacific Ocean
proposals.motivation: Marine dissolved organic matter (DOM) constitutes a massive carbon reservoir comparable in size to atmospheric CO2, with its long-lived, biorefractory fraction central to carbon sequestration. The prevailing paradigm, based largely on bacterial culture studies, posits that bacteria are the primary architects of this refractory DOM via the 'microbial carbon pump'. However, the potential role of archaea, particularly abundant in the deep ocean and in oligotrophic gyres like the North Pacific, remains largely unquantified, creating a critical gap in our understanding of the biological controls on the ocean carbon cycle.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that planktonic archaea, specifically marine group II Thaumarchaeota, are significant producers of biorefractory DOM compounds, and that their contribution rivals or exceeds that of bacteria in oligotrophic surface and mesopelagic waters of the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre.
proposals.method: We will conduct shipboard incubation experiments at Station ALOHA using size-fractionation and metabolic inhibitors to selectively suppress bacterial vs. archaeal activity in surface and deep chlorophyll maximum waters. DOM chemical characterisation will employ ultrahigh-resolution Fourier Transform Ion Cyclotron Resonance Mass Spectrometry (FT-ICR MS) to track the production of molecular formulas associated with recalcitrance. Concurrent metatranscriptomic analysis of active microbial communities will link specific taxonomic groups (using tools like MetaBAT2 for binning) to the expression of genes involved in DOM precursor synthesis and modification, such as those for membrane lipid and exopolymer production.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative assessment of archaeal versus bacterial contributions to refractory DOM production in a key oligotrophic biome. It will refine the conceptual model of the microbial carbon pump by identifying the specific microbial actors and their metabolic signatures, thereby improving the parameterization of carbon sequestration in global ocean biogeochemical models under climate change scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has established bacteria's role, systematically testing archaea as a major source is novel. Combining inhibitor-based activity partitioning with FT-ICR MS molecular fingerprinting and metatranscriptomics directly links microbial identity to the synthesis of persistent carbon compounds, a methodological advance beyond correlative studies.

proposals.title: Linking heterotrophic bacterial community structure to the production of biorefractory amino sugars in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre
proposals.motivation: The marine dissolved organic matter (DOM) pool contains a significant fraction of biorefractory compounds, such as amino sugars, which persist for millennia and represent a major carbon reservoir. The specific microbial sources and processes that generate this recalcitrant material remain poorly characterized, creating a critical gap in our understanding of the ocean's biological carbon pump and long-term carbon sequestration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, phylogenetically coherent groups of heterotrophic bacteria in the oligotrophic ocean are responsible for the net production of biorefractory amino sugars, and that their activity is linked to distinct metabolic strategies for resource acquisition under nutrient limitation.
proposals.method: We will conduct size-fractionated incubation experiments with 13C-labeled substrates at Station ALOHA, tracking the incorporation of label into dissolved amino sugars via NMR and mass spectrometry. Concurrently, we will use 16S rRNA gene sequencing and fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) to identify active bacterial taxa. A novel application of network analysis (e.g., SPIEC-EASI or MENA) will statistically link specific bacterial clades (e.g., SAR11, Roseobacter, Bacteroidetes) to the production dynamics of individual amino sugar compounds, controlling for abiotic factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, taxon-specific links between marine heterotrophic bacteria and the synthesis of key biorefractory biochemicals. It will advance mechanistic models of DOM cycling by identifying microbial sources of recalcitrance, thereby refining estimates of carbon turnover and sequestration in the oligotrophic ocean.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has catalogued DOM composition and microbial community structure, this study innovatively applies isotopic tracing and microbial network analysis to directly attribute the production of a specific class of biorefractory compounds to identifiable bacterial taxa, moving beyond correlation to mechanism.

proposals.title: Productivity Regimes and Microbial Substrate Selection: Net Removal Rates of Neutral Sugars and Amino Acids Across the North Pacific Ocean
proposals.motivation: Dissolved organic matter (DOM) is a critical reservoir in the ocean's carbon cycle, yet the factors controlling the turnover of its labile constituents, like neutral sugars and amino acids, are poorly constrained at an ecosystem level. Understanding how microbial processing of these key substrates differs between high- and low-productivity regimes is essential for predicting carbon sequestration and nutrient regeneration, especially under climate-driven shifts in ocean stratification and productivity. This directly addresses themes in Karl's recent work on microbial genome size selection and phytoplankton resilience in oligotrophic systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that net removal rates of labile DOM components (neutral sugars and amino acids) will be faster in high-productivity regions due to higher microbial biomass and activity, but that the relative removal of these compound classes will shift between regimes, reflecting different microbial metabolic strategies and substrate quality.
proposals.method: We will conduct shipboard incubation experiments using seawater from contrasting productivity regimes along a North Pacific transect (e.g., subarctic to subtropical gyre). Seawater will be filtered (0.2 µm) to create particle-free inoculum, amended with isotopically labeled (13C/15N) substrates (a mix of neutral sugars and amino acids at environmentally relevant concentrations), and incubated in the dark at in-situ temperature. Subsamples will be taken over 24-72 hours. Concentrations of target compounds will be quantified via high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) coupled to fluorescence and mass spectrometry detection. Net removal rates will be calculated from the decay of added substrates, corrected with killed controls.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, comparative measurements of labile DOM component turnover across major oceanic productivity gradients, quantifying a key flux in marine biogeochemistry. It will link microbial substrate processing to ecosystem state, informing models of carbon export and nitrogen regeneration, particularly relevant to global N2 fixation estimates which depend on DOM stoichiometry. The results will test the concept of microbial metabolic resilience in oligotrophic oceans.
proposals.novelty: While DOM composition is studied, simultaneous, rate-based comparison of specific labile compound classes across defined productivity regimes is novel. It moves beyond cataloging DOM pools to mechanistically testing how microbial community function, shaped by environmental selection, governs the fate of carbon and nitrogen.

proposals.title: Experimental perturbation of marine bacterial communities with defined biorefractory DOM: Testing the genomic and metabolic resilience of oligotrophic microbiomes
proposals.motivation: The vast pool of dissolved organic matter (DOM) in the oligotrophic ocean is predominantly biorefractory, yet its molecular composition and role in shaping microbial community structure and metabolic function remain poorly understood. Pre-2026 work, including Karl's research on microbial genome size and phytoplankton resilience, highlights adaptations to nutrient scarcity, but the direct impact of specific refractory biochemicals on bacterial growth and community dynamics is untested. This knowledge gap limits our ability to predict how the marine carbon cycle responds to perturbations that may alter DOM composition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the addition of specific, chemically-defined biorefractory DOM compounds (e.g., humic acid derivatives, specific lignin-like or melanoidin-like polymers) will selectively stimulate the growth of bacterial taxa with larger genomes and broader metabolic repertoires, consistent with the genomic streamlining theory, and will alter community-level nitrogen and carbon utilization efficiencies.
proposals.method: We will conduct shipboard incubation experiments in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre, amending seawater with a suite of 13C/15N-labeled, defined biorefractory compounds at environmentally relevant concentrations. Bacterial community response will be tracked via high-throughput 16S rRNA and metagenomic sequencing over time. Growth responses, carbon incorporation, and changes in functional gene abundance (e.g., for transporter systems, oxidative enzymes) will be quantified using NanoSIMS coupled with fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) of taxonomically identified cells and differential coverage binning of metagenomic assemblies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental link between specific biorefractory DOM molecules and the genomic traits of responsive bacterial populations, directly testing abiotic selection for genome size. It will yield novel metrics for the 'resilience' of oligotrophic microbiomes to DOM perturbations and refine estimates of carbon sequestration pathways by identifying which refractory compounds can be metabolically accessed and under what conditions.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies correlate DOM fields with microbial taxonomy, this proposal uniquely employs a reverse-ecology approach, experimentally adding defined refractory biochemicals to test causal relationships, moving beyond correlation to mechanism. It directly integrates Karl's themes of genome size selection and biogeochemical feedbacks with modern molecular tools.

proposals.title: Disentangling Iron Supply from Physical and Nitrogen Cycle Drivers of Atlantic-Pacific Ocean Biogeochemical Contrasts
proposals.motivation: The Atlantic and Pacific Oceans exhibit stark differences in surface nutrient inventories and biological productivity, with the North Atlantic being more productive and having lower N:P ratios than the North Pacific. Pre-2026 understanding attributes this to a complex interplay of iron supply from dust, basin-scale circulation, mixing regimes, and nitrogen loss processes like denitrification. Resolving the primary driver is critical for accurately modeling ocean carbon sequestration and predicting biogeochemical feedbacks to climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that inter-basin differences in external iron supply and bioavailability are the primary determinant of observed Atlantic-Pacific contrasts in phytoplankton community structure and nitrogen cycling, exerting a stronger control than differences in physical circulation, vertical mixing, or nitrogen loss processes.
proposals.method: We will leverage and expand the Global Oceanic Diazotroph Database (version 2) to analyze basin-scale patterns in N2 fixation, a key iron-sensitive process. Concurrently, we will apply a novel comparative metagenomic framework to microbial communities from the North Atlantic and North Pacific, quantifying genomic traits (e.g., iron-stress genes, nitrogenase abundance, genome size) as bio-indicators of environmental selection. Statistical modeling (e.g., structural equation modeling) will be used to partition the variance in these biological metrics among predictors: satellite-derived dust deposition (iron proxy), physical reanalysis data (mixing, circulation indices), and observed nitrite maxima (denitrification proxy).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, trait-based assessment of the factors controlling ocean basin biogeochemistry, moving beyond correlative studies. It will directly test the iron hypothesis against competing physical and nitrogen-cycle explanations, refining global models of marine nitrogen fixation and carbon export. The findings will inform projections of how these biogeochemical provinces may respond to future changes in dust deposition and ocean stratification.
proposals.novelty: While iron limitation is known, its primacy over well-established physical and nitrogen-loss drivers in explaining basin-scale contrasts is unresolved. The novel integration of a global biogeochemical database with microbial genomic trait analysis offers a direct, biological lens to test driver importance, moving beyond purely geochemical comparisons.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Role of Water Column Denitrification in Establishing the Atlantic-Pacific Phosphorus Contrast: A Microbial and Biogeochemical Assessment
proposals.motivation: The Atlantic Ocean exhibits significantly higher dissolved inorganic phosphate (DIP) concentrations than the Pacific at equivalent depths, a fundamental biogeochemical contrast with major implications for global carbon cycling and ecosystem productivity. Pre-2026 hypotheses attribute this to greater water column denitrification (and associated phosphate release) in the expansive oxygen minimum zones (OMZs) of the Pacific, but a basin-scale, process-rate-based quantification is lacking. This gap limits our ability to predict how climate-driven deoxygenation may alter oceanic nutrient inventories and feedbacks.
proposals.hypothesis: Enhanced water column denitrification in the Pacific Ocean, particularly in its major OMZs, is a primary driver of the observed inter-basin phosphorus contrast by preferentially removing fixed nitrogen and increasing the relative availability of phosphate (lowering N:P ratios).
proposals.method: We will integrate field observations, rate measurements, and modeling. New and archived hydrographic/nutrient data (e.g., GO-SHIP, GEOTRACES) will be used to map N* anomalies (N* = [NO3-] - 16*[PO43-] + 2.9) as a tracer of net denitrification. Direct measurements of denitrification rates (via 15N isotope tracer techniques) and N2O concentrations will be conducted on cruises targeting Pacific OMZs (e.g., Eastern Tropical North and South Pacific). A coupled 1-D biogeochemical model, incorporating microbial functional groups (diazotrophs, denitrifiers) and parameterized with our rate data, will be used to scale local processes to basin budgets and quantify the net phosphate accumulation effect.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first basin-scale, rate-constrained estimate of water column denitrification's contribution to the Atlantic-Pacific phosphorus difference. It will refine global ocean nitrogen and phosphorus budgets and improve the representation of these cycles in Earth system models. The findings will directly inform predictions of how ongoing ocean deoxygenation may shift nutrient stoichiometry and primary production patterns.
proposals.novelty: While denitrification is known to affect N:P ratios, this study uniquely quantifies its basin-scale impact on the canonical Atlantic-Pacific phosphate contrast by synthesizing novel rate measurements, microbial process data, and tracer distributions, moving beyond correlation to mechanistic, quantitative attribution.

proposals.title: In situ molecular diagnosis of iron and phosphorus co-limitation in North Pacific subtropical gyre diazotrophs
proposals.motivation: Marine nitrogen fixation, a key control on ocean productivity and carbon sequestration, is primarily limited by iron (Fe) and phosphorus (P) availability. However, direct, in situ assessments of nutrient stress in diazotrophic microbes are lacking, hindering our ability to predict how N2 fixation will respond to climate-driven changes in nutrient supply. Current global estimates, like those from the Global Oceanic Diazotroph Database, rely on indirect proxies and bottle incubations, which may not reflect in situ physiological states.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific gene expression biomarkers for Fe and P stress, previously validated in culture, can diagnose the in situ nutrient status of key diazotrophic symbioses (e.g., Richelia associated with diatoms) and free-living unicellular cyanobacteria in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre, revealing spatial and temporal patterns of co-limitation.
proposals.method: We will conduct high-resolution transects across Fe and P gradients in the North Pacific using the Hawaii Ocean Time-series framework. RNA will be extracted from size-fractionated samples targeting key diazotrophs. Using quantitative PCR and targeted metatranscriptomics, we will assay expression of established biomarker genes: the Fe-stress indicator isiB (flavodoxin) and the P-stress indicator phoA (alkaline phosphatase) and pstS (high-affinity phosphate transporter). Expression ratios will be calibrated against concurrent nutrient addition bioassays and nutrient concentration data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, molecularly-based map of nutrient stress for oceanic diazotrophs, moving beyond bulk rate measurements to a physiological diagnosis. It will test the resilience of phytoplankton to nutrient stress under climate change and deliver a novel constraint for biogeochemical models, improving predictions of future oceanic nitrogen and carbon cycles.
proposals.novelty: While nutrient stress biomarkers are known, their systematic application to diagnose in situ co-limitation in specific, globally significant diazotrophs in the open ocean is novel. This bridges microbial ecology's molecular tools with global biogeochemistry's need for mechanistic understanding of nutrient controls.

proposals.title: Microbial Resource Competition in the Oligotrophic Ocean: Iron and Phosphorus Dynamics Structuring Diazotroph and Non-Diazotroph Assemblages
proposals.motivation: Marine dinitrogen (N2) fixation, primarily performed by diazotrophic microbes, is a critical source of bioavailable nitrogen that supports ocean productivity and carbon export. However, the factors controlling the distribution and activity of diazotrophs versus competing non-diazotrophs remain poorly resolved at the community interaction level. Given that climate change is projected to alter nutrient stoichiometry and supply (e.g., via increased stratification and dust deposition), understanding the bottom-up controls of these microbial groups is essential for predicting future ocean biogeochemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the outcome of competition for iron (Fe) and phosphorus (P) between diazotrophs and non-diazotrophs is a primary determinant of microbial community structure in oligotrophic regions, with diazotroph dominance requiring a Fe:P supply ratio significantly higher than the canonical Redfield ratio.
proposals.method: We will conduct a combination of field experiments and modeling. Shipboard incubation experiments in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre will manipulate Fe and P concentrations (single and co-limitation treatments) in natural microbial communities, tracking population dynamics of key diazotrophs (e.g., *Crocosphaera*, *Trichodesmium*) and dominant non-diazotrophs via quantitative PCR and flow cytometry. Concurrent measurements of N2 fixation (15N2 uptake), nutrient drawdown, and bulk community metatranscriptomics will link resource use to function. A resource-competition model, based on the classic Monod kinetics framework, will be parameterized with our experimental data to predict community structure across gradients of Fe and P supply.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, trait-based framework for predicting diazotroph biogeography and N2 fixation rates based on trace metal and macronutrient supply. It will directly inform and constrain global biogeochemical models that currently use simplified representations of diazotrophy. The results will refine estimates of oceanic nitrogen inputs and their sensitivity to environmental change.
proposals.novelty: While the high Fe and P demands of diazotrophy are known, this study uniquely focuses on the *competitive outcome* at the community level under dual nutrient limitation, moving beyond correlations or single-strain physiology to a predictive ecological framework.

proposals.title: Millennial-scale modulation of atmospheric CO2 by iron-mediated variations in oceanic nitrogen fixation: A modeling synthesis
proposals.motivation: The biological pump's strength is fundamentally limited by nitrogen availability in vast ocean regions. Pre-2026 work, including the Global Oceanic Diazotroph Database, establishes iron as a key control on marine N2 fixation, but the long-term (millennial-scale) carbon sequestration consequences of this link remain poorly quantified. Understanding this feedback is critical for interpreting past glacial-interglacial CO2 cycles and projecting the ocean's future carbon sink capacity under climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that variations in iron dust flux to the ocean, by modulating the rate and distribution of marine N2 fixation over millennia, are a primary driver of changes in the oceanic biological carbon pump and consequent atmospheric CO2 drawdown.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel Earth System Model of Intermediate Complexity (EMIC) framework, integrating a dynamic global diazotroph module (parameterized from the GODDv2 database) with explicit iron limitation. The model will be forced with reconstructed paleo-dust fluxes (e.g., from ice cores) over the last 800,000 years. We will employ a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm to constrain key unknown parameters (e.g., Fe:C ratios in diazotrophs, C export efficiency) by fitting simulated atmospheric CO2 and δ15N sediment records to paleo-proxy data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic, quantitative assessment of the diazotroph-iron-carbon coupling over glacial cycles. It will yield a novel, testable estimate of the carbon sequestration efficiency of oceanic N2 fixation and clarify its role relative to other proposed mechanisms (e.g., Southern Ocean ventilation) in driving past CO2 variability.
proposals.novelty: While iron-diazotroph links are known, their explicit integration into a millennial-scale carbon cycle model to directly quantify atmospheric CO2 impact is novel. This moves beyond correlative studies to a process-based, mechanistic synthesis of Karl's long-term themes of ocean nitrogen, carbon, and microbial ecology.

proposals.title: Mesoscale Physical Forcing of the Iron-Phosphate Relationship in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre: Implications for Nitrogen Fixation and Carbon Export
proposals.motivation: The North Pacific Subtropical Gyre (NPSG) is a globally significant, nitrogen-limited biome where the availability of iron (Fe) and phosphate (PO4) co-limits the activity of diazotrophs, setting the rate of new nitrogen input and carbon export. Pre-2026 observations show large sub-basin variability in the Fe:PO4 ratio, but the physical drivers of this heterogeneity are poorly resolved, limiting our ability to predict biogeochemical feedbacks to climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mesoscale eddies and frontal zones in the NPSG physically modulate the subsurface supply and stoichiometry of Fe and PO4, creating localized hotspots and coldspots for diazotroph activity that are not captured by basin-scale averages.
proposals.method: We will conduct a targeted field campaign in the NPSG, sampling across cyclonic and anticyclonic eddies and frontal zones identified using satellite altimetry (AVISO). High-resolution vertical profiles will be obtained for dissolved Fe (using a trace-metal clean rosette), PO4, and nitrogen fixation rates (via 15N2 incubations). Concurrent metagenomic and metatranscriptomic analyses of microbial communities will assess diazotroph genome content and activity. Data will be integrated using a Lagrangian framework to trace water mass history and quantify physical-biological coupling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, process-based understanding of how mesoscale physics controls the Fe-PO4 relationship, directly informing models of oceanic nitrogen fixation. It will yield a refined, spatially explicit estimate of N2 fixation for the NPSG and test the resilience of phytoplankton communities to stoichiometric variability under climate change.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has mapped global diazotroph distributions and basin-scale nutrient fields, this study uniquely targets the explicit physical modulation of the critical Fe:PO4 stoichiometry at the submesoscale to mesoscale, linking instantaneous physical forcing to microbial genome content and function.

proposals.title: Decadal-scale variability in mesopelagic carbon flux in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre: Linking microbial community resilience to climate-driven export dynamics
proposals.motivation: The ocean's biological carbon pump, which transports organic carbon from the surface to the deep sea, is a critical climate regulator, yet its long-term variability in oligotrophic gyres remains poorly constrained. Pre-2026 studies, including those on phytoplankton resilience, suggest that climate change may alter export efficiency, but sustained observations in the critical mesopelagic 'twilight zone' (100-1000m) are rare, creating a major gap in predicting biogeochemical feedbacks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that interannual to decadal variability in physical forcing (e.g., ENSO, Pacific Decadal Oscillation) drives coherent changes in carbon export flux into the mesopelagic zone at Station ALOHA, and that these changes are moderated by the resilience and genomic traits of the underlying microbial community, including diazotrophs.
proposals.method: We will establish a sustained time-series by augmenting the ongoing Hawaii Ocean Time-series (HOT) program with monthly deployments of Neutrally Buoyant Sediment Traps (NBSTs) at 150m, 300m, and 500m depths. Sequentially collected trap material will be analyzed for particulate organic carbon/nitrogen flux and subjected to metagenomic sequencing. Time-series analysis will correlate flux variability with HOT's physical/biological parameters and climate indices. We will apply the genomic size-selection framework from Karl's prior work to test for links between microbial genome streamlining in trap communities and flux magnitude/persistence.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first multi-year, depth-resolved carbon flux dataset from the oligotrophic North Pacific mesopelagic, directly testing theories of phytoplankton resilience. It will quantitatively link climate oscillations to carbon sequestration processes and evaluate how microbial community assembly, including diazotrophs from the global database, governs the fate of exported carbon.
proposals.novelty: While HOT provides seminal surface data, this proposal uniquely targets the under-sampled mesopelagic with a sustained trap program, integrating physical, biogeochemical, and genomic time-series to mechanistically connect climate forcing, export flux, and microbial adaptive strategies.

proposals.title: Emergent Carbon Transfer Efficiency: Modeling Microbial Network Interactions in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre
proposals.motivation: Oceanic microbial communities mediate the biological carbon pump, yet the efficiency of carbon transfer through these networks remains a major uncertainty in global biogeochemical models. Traditional bulk parameterizations fail to capture how specific interactions—like cross-feeding, competition, and viral lysis—govern the fate of carbon in oligotrophic systems like the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre. A mechanistic, network-based understanding is critical for predicting how these ecosystems, and their feedbacks to climate, will respond to environmental change.
proposals.hypothesis: Carbon transfer efficiency (CTE) in oligotrophic surface oceans is an emergent property of microbial network structure and interaction strength, with higher network modularity and specific symbiotic exchanges leading to greater carbon retention in the particulate pool.
proposals.method: We will construct dynamic, trait-based network models of microbial communities using metabolic theory and genomic trait data (e.g., from the Global Ocean Reference Genomes and the global oceanic diazotroph database). The model will integrate key functional groups (diazotrophs, phytoplankton, heterotrophic bacteria, archaea, and viruses) and their interactions (mutualism, competition, predation). We will use a hybrid modeling architecture, combining ordinary differential equations for population dynamics with agent-based rules for stochastic interaction events, implemented in a tool like MIMICS or a custom framework in R/Python. The model will be parameterized and validated against time-series data from Station ALOHA, including microbial abundance, metabolic rates, and particulate organic carbon flux.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, mechanistic framework linking microbial community structure to ecosystem-scale carbon flux, moving beyond black-box efficiency parameters. It will provide specific, testable predictions on how shifts in diazotroph abundance or viral pressure alter carbon sequestration potential. The results will directly inform next-generation Earth system models seeking to incorporate microbial network dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies correlate microbial diversity with function, this proposal explicitly models CTE as an emergent outcome of simulated interactions, bridging the gap between genomic potential and ecosystem-scale biogeochemistry. It uniquely integrates diazotrophy and viral processes into a unified network model of carbon processing for oligotrophic oceans.

proposals.title: A Global Atlas of Marine Phosphate Stress: Mapping Microbial Physiological Status via APase-Corrected Nucleotide Ratios
proposals.motivation: Phosphorus (P) availability is a key regulator of ocean productivity and carbon cycling, but direct measurements of dissolved inorganic phosphate (DIP) fail to capture microbial physiological stress, especially in oligotrophic regions where DIP is below detection. Pre-2026 work, including the APase correction method (using alkaline phosphatase activity to estimate bioavailable P), has shown promise in revealing P stress from single-cell nucleotide (e.g., dATP:ATP) ratios, yet these insights remain confined to limited field studies. Scaling this approach is critical to understand the global biogeography of P limitation and its feedbacks on nitrogen fixation and carbon export.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying the APase-corrected nucleotide ratio method across global oceanographic transects will reveal extensive, previously undetected regions of phosphate stress, particularly in the subtropical gyres and the North Pacific, and that this stress pattern will be a primary driver of spatial variability in microbial genome size and diazotroph activity.
proposals.method: We will implement the APase correction protocol on samples from large-scale surveys, including the GEOTRACES and GO-SHIP programs, focusing on the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre. Seawater samples will be analyzed for bulk and single-cell dATP:ATP ratios via enzymatic assays and HPLC. Concurrent measurements of alkaline phosphatase activity (using MUF-phosphate) will correct ratios for internal nucleotide pools, generating a 'Phosphate Stress Index' (PSI). This index will be spatially interpolated using objective analysis and correlated with concurrent measurements of microbial community structure (via 16S/18S rRNA gene sequencing), N2 fixation rates (acetylene reduction/15N2), and particulate organic carbon.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first global, high-resolution map of microbial phosphate stress, directly linking microbial physiology to ocean biogeochemistry. It will quantitatively constrain the role of P stress in regulating diazotroph distributions and nitrogen fixation, thereby improving models of oceanic nutrient limitation. The dataset will serve as a benchmark for validating satellite-derived proxies of nutrient stress and assessing climate change impacts on oligotrophic ecosystems.
proposals.novelty: While the APase-nucleotide method exists, its systematic application to generate a global biogeochemical atlas is novel. This moves beyond correlative DIP measurements to a direct, physiological mapping of resource stress, integrating Karl lab themes of microbial adaptation, global nutrient cycles, and ecosystem resilience in a unified framework.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Deep Ocean and Microbial Blind Spot: A Biogeochemical Assessment of Satellite-Derived Biological Statistics
proposals.motivation: Satellite ocean color is the primary tool for estimating global ocean primary production and phytoplankton biomass, yet it fundamentally integrates only the surface ocean's optical signal. This creates major, unquantified biases for deep chlorophyll maxima (DCMs), sub-mesoscale phenomena, and the vast microbial world, including picoplankton and nitrogen-fixing diazotrophs, which are critical to carbon export and the marine nitrogen cycle. These biases propagate into Earth system models, potentially misrepresenting ocean biogeochemical feedbacks to climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that satellite-derived estimates of primary production and phytoplankton biomass systematically underestimate total integrated biomass and activity in oligotrophic gyres by failing to account for contributions from the deep chlorophyll maximum and from microbial functional groups with distinct optical signatures.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Station ALOHA time-series in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre, combining three decades of in-situ biogeochemical profiles (nutrients, pigments, carbon fixation), high-resolution flow cytometry, and genomic data (including from the Global Oceanic Diazotroph Database v2) with concurrent satellite data (MODIS, VIIRS). We will develop a bias-correction framework using machine learning (specifically, a gradient-boosted regression tree model) trained on in-situ integrated water column properties to predict the discrepancy between satellite-surface and in-situ-depth-resolved estimates of chlorophyll, primary production, and diazotroph abundance.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitatively constrained, regional-scale estimate of the 'missing' biological carbon and nitrogen flux in the oligotrophic ocean due to satellite limitations. It will provide a validated correction algorithm to improve satellite-derived global N2 fixation and carbon cycle estimates, directly informing next-generation biogeochemical models.
proposals.novelty: While satellite limitations are acknowledged, this study uniquely integrates Karl lab's long-term microbial and biogeochemical time-series with modern data science to explicitly quantify the bias for key functional groups (diazotrophs, picoplankton) and the DCM, moving beyond qualitative critique to a predictive framework.

proposals.title: Integrating physiological thresholds and microbial symbioses to project extinction risk for non-migratory corals in the North Pacific under climate change
proposals.motivation: Coral reefs are non-migratory, foundation species facing unprecedented stress from ocean warming and acidification, which threaten their physiological limits and the stability of their essential microbial symbioses. Given the critical role of corals in marine nitrogen and carbon cycling, their potential extinction represents a major risk to ocean biogeochemistry and ecosystem resilience, particularly in oligotrophic regions like the North Pacific. Current risk assessments often lack integration of microbial community dynamics and organism-specific physiological thresholds.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the extinction risk for non-migratory corals is determined by the intersection of host physiological thresholds for calcification under acidification and the resilience of their core microbial symbionts, particularly diazotrophs, to thermal stress.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Global Oceanic Diazotroph Database (version 2) and existing physiological datasets to model taxon-specific thermal and pH tolerance windows for key North Pacific coral species. Using a trait-based modeling framework, we will integrate these abiotic limits with genomic and metabolic network models of associated microbial communities (informed by principles of abiotic genome size selection) to simulate symbiosis stability. Extinction probability will be projected under IPCC CMIP5 climate scenarios for 2100 using a Bayesian hierarchical model that accounts for regional heterogeneity in the Pacific.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, mechanistic framework for assessing extinction risk in sessile marine organisms by explicitly linking host physiology and microbial symbiosis function. It will deliver quantitative, spatially explicit projections of coral vulnerability in the North Pacific, directly informing conservation priorities. The findings will advance understanding of how microbial partnerships moderate ecosystem responses to climate change.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its explicit integration of microbial symbiont community resilience—a key moderator of host physiology—into classical species distribution and extinction risk models for corals. It applies concepts from global genomic analyses of marine microbes to solve a pressing conservation biogeography problem.

proposals.title: Resolving the Ocean's Microbial Carbon Pump: Integrating Satellite Ocean Color with Long-Term Biogeochemical Time-Series to Quantify Climate Feedbacks
proposals.motivation: The oligotrophic gyres of the Pacific and Atlantic are vast, nitrogen-limited ecosystems where microbial communities regulate the ocean's biological carbon pump. Pre-2026 understanding of their response to climate variability is fragmented, relying on either spatially extensive but shallow satellite proxies or deep but geographically limited in-situ time-series. A holistic analysis is critical to predict how phytoplankton resilience and microbial genome streamlining, as revealed by Karl's recent work, modulate carbon export and nitrogen fixation under changing environmental conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating satellite-derived phytoplankton functional types (PFTs) with deep biogeochemical time-series data will reveal coherent, basin-scale patterns in microbial community structure and metabolic function (e.g., carbon fixation, nitrogen fixation) that are not apparent from either dataset alone, and that these patterns are predictable from environmental forcing.
proposals.method: We will fuse multi-sensor satellite ocean color data (SeaWiFS, MODIS, VIIRS) processed with the Garver-Siegel-Maritorena (GSM) model to generate time-series of PFTs and particulate organic carbon. These will be statistically integrated with in-situ data from the Hawaii Ocean Time-series (HOT) and Bermuda Atlantic Time-series Study (BATS), including nutrients, microbial abundances, N2 fixation rates (from the Global Oceanic Diazotroph Database v2), and carbon export. A machine learning framework (Random Forest regression) will be used to identify the key satellite and environmental predictors of in-situ microbial processes and to create regional predictive maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, observationally constrained framework for quantifying the spatial and temporal coherence of the microbial carbon pump in oligotrophic oceans. It will directly test the hypothesized link between microbial genome size, community structure from space, and biogeochemical rate processes, providing a more robust and elevated estimate of regional carbon and nitrogen cycling feedbacks to climate.
proposals.novelty: While satellite-in situ comparisons exist, this proposal uniquely focuses on integrating multi-decadal satellite PFTs with deep biogeochemical rate measurements (N2 fixation, export) to mechanistically link optical signatures with microbial metabolic strategies and carbon sequestration, a synthesis not yet achieved in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Reconstructing Quaternary Ocean Circulation Shifts Using the Genomic Footprint of Diazotroph Biogeography and Population Structure
proposals.motivation: Historical changes in ocean circulation, such as shifts in the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre, are critical for understanding past climate-carbon cycle feedbacks, but are poorly constrained by sparse physical proxy records. Microbial species, particularly globally significant nitrogen-fixing diazotrophs like *Trichodesmium* and symbioses with diatoms, serve as biogeochemical sentinels whose present-day distributions and genetic diversity encode signatures of past dispersal and isolation driven by circulation. This project leverages the emerging global database of marine microbes to address a core inverse problem in paleoceanography.
proposals.hypothesis: The contemporary population genomic structure (e.g., single nucleotide polymorphism patterns) and biogeographic boundaries of key marine diazotrophs retain a quantifiable signal of major ocean circulation changes during the late Quaternary, specifically the intensification or weakening of gyral boundaries and meridional overturning.
proposals.method: We will integrate the Global Oceanic Diazotroph Database version 2 (GODDv2) with existing and newly collated population genomic datasets for target species from the North Pacific and other basins. A Bayesian Approximate Bayesian Computation (ABC) framework will be employed, where forward simulations of population genetics under alternative, physically-plausible historical circulation scenarios (e.g., altered gyre strength) are compared to observed genomic summary statistics. The model will couple a simplified ocean particle-tracking model (for connectivity) with a population genetic simulator (e.g., using *ms* or *fastsimcoal2* architectures) to identify the circulation history that maximizes the likelihood of the observed genetic data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, biologically-informed reconstruction of Quaternary circulation changes, complementing and testing physical proxy records. It will demonstrate a new methodology for using microbial genomic data as a paleoceanographic proxy, directly linking the fields of microbial biogeography, population genetics, and physical oceanography. The results will provide critical context for interpreting the resilience of oligotrophic ecosystems, like the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre, to past climate forcing.
proposals.novelty: While species distributions are used to infer present-day currents, inverting population genetics to deduce *historical* physical circulation is a novel, non-obvious application of microbial data. This approach moves beyond correlative biogeography to a mechanistic, model-based test of paleo-circulation hypotheses using genetic drift and gene flow as integrated tracers.

ideas.title: Eddy-mediated biogeographic shifts: Modeling terebellid worm range dynamics under mesoscale nitrate flux regimes
ideas.description: This research fuses biogeographic modeling with mesoscale physical oceanography to predict how the ranges of terebellid polychaetes will shift under climate change. It specifically investigates how mesoscale eddies, by laterally transporting isotopically distinct nitrate pools, create dynamic mosaics of benthic food web baselines and primary productivity that define suitable habitat. The novel model integrates high-resolution eddy-resolving physical-biogeochemical simulations with species distribution models to project how the frequency, intensity, and pathways of these eddies will modulate both larval connectivity and adult habitat quality. This reveals future 'eddy refugia' and 'eddy stress corridors' that are invisible to models using only mean environmental conditions.

ideas.title: Integrated Lagrangian Particle Tracking with Process-Attributing Biogeochemical Tracers
ideas.description: This approach embeds virtual particles within a high-resolution ocean circulation model that is dynamically coupled to a biogeochemical module simulating the evolution of δ¹⁵N and related tracers (e.g., N*, phosphate stoichiometry). As particles are tracked backward from the observed low-δ¹⁵N water, the model continuously updates their simulated isotopic signature based on the encountered physical and biogeochemical environment. This creates a probabilistic attribution of the water mass origin not just to a geographic location, but to the specific nitrogen cycle process (e.g., N₂ fixation) dominant along its pathway, significantly strengthening causal inference beyond trajectory analysis alone.

ideas.title: Modeling the Spatiotemporal Niche of the Low-δ¹⁵N Core Using Species Distribution Frameworks
ideas.description: This research fuses repeated transect sampling of a low-δ¹⁵N isotopic signal with ecological niche modeling (ENM) techniques. The low-δ¹⁵N core is treated as a distinct 'ecological entity,' and its observed presences/absences across seasons and years are used to calibrate species distribution models. By modeling the core's distribution against concurrent environmental layers (e.g., temperature, nutrients, currents), the project predicts its full spatiotemporal niche, identifying where and when it is likely to manifest beyond sampled transects. This approach reveals the dynamic habitat of the core and pinpoints the key seasonal and interannual drivers controlling its biogeochemical signature.

ideas.title: Apply Sedimentary Provenance Tracer Techniques to Marine Nitrogen Particle Flux
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from sedimentary provenance analysis. In that field, the source of sediments is traced by comparing isotopic fingerprints of source rocks and transported deposits. The adapted solution treats the dissolved nitrate pool as the source and sinking PON as the transported material. By measuring δ¹⁵N of nitrate and δ¹⁵N of size-fractionated, depth-resolved sinking particles, the isotopic offset becomes a quantitative tracer. This allows us to deconvolve the integrated 'transport history'—the suite of nitrogen transformations like assimilation, remineralization, and grazing—that occurs between the source (nitrate) and the sink (exported particles), moving beyond a simple comparison to a process-constraining framework.

ideas.title: Tracing the origin of a low-δ¹⁵N signal using forensic source apportionment
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a low-δ¹⁵N core in a sedimentary or biogeochemical system originates from remote nitrogen fixation. By drawing an analogy from environmental forensics, where distinguishing between distant pollution sources and local re-emission is a common problem, we adapt the solution of multi-tracer fingerprinting. The analogous field solves this by using independent, process-specific tracers (e.g., compound-specific isotopes, trace element ratios) to statistically apportion sources. Adapted to this context, the research would identify and measure a complementary geochemical tracer—such as a specific amino acid δ¹⁵N, a biomarker ratio, or a sediment redox proxy—that is inherently tied to local nitrogen recycling processes (e.g., ammonification, dissimilatory nitrate reduction). By analyzing the covariance of this local process tracer with the bulk low-δ¹⁵N signal across spatial or temporal gradients, one can test the hypothesis that the low-δ¹⁵N core is generated in situ, thereby inverting the presumed source from remote fixation to local recycling.

ideas.title: Applying Microbial Source Tracking (MST) Frameworks to Map Diazotroph Provenance and Transport
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Microbial Source Tracking (MST) in environmental hydrology to the SSCC context. In MST, host-specific genetic markers are used to trace the origin of fecal bacteria in water systems back to specific animal or human sources. The adapted research idea applies this framework by treating different stages or components of the SSCC pathway (e.g., source soil, compost feedstock, finished compost, amended soil) as distinct microbial 'source' reservoirs. The goal is to develop and apply compartment-specific genetic markers for diazotrophs (e.g., based on nifH gene variants or associated genomic signatures) to map these communities. This will allow researchers to quantitatively track the provenance, dispersal, and intermixing of nitrogen-fixing bacteria along the SSCC pathway, transforming community mapping into a source-attribution model that can identify which diazotrophs in a final amended soil originated from which upstream SSCC component.

ideas.title: Integrating Regional Oceanic Nitrate δ¹⁵N Data into a Global Budget Using a Hierarchical Bayesian Framework
ideas.description: This research idea proposes to scale up Pacific-specific findings on nitrate δ¹⁵N to a global oceanic budget by drawing an analogy from climate science's solution to building a global carbon budget. The analogous problem—integrating heterogeneous, uncertain regional flux data into a coherent global whole—was solved using a hierarchical Bayesian data assimilation framework. Adapting this solution, the research will develop a global model for oceanic nitrate δ¹⁵N that formally incorporates the Pacific data as a key regional prior within a hierarchical Bayesian structure. The model will assimilate all available regional δ¹⁵N measurements and known process constraints, treating the global ocean's nitrogen isotope mass balance as the ultimate boundary condition. This approach will quantitatively propagate uncertainties, identify regions or processes where current estimates are inconsistent with the global picture, and produce a probabilistically robust, globally scaled nitrogen isotope budget.

ideas.title: Geometric morphometrics of diazotroph-associated structures to link microbial function and host evolution
ideas.description: This research applies geometric morphometrics, a high-resolution shape analysis technique, to quantify the three-dimensional morphology of invertebrate structures known to host nitrogen-fixing diazotrophs, such as the chaetae of terebellid worms or the root nodules of legumes. Concurrently, molecular techniques (e.g., amplicon sequencing, metagenomics) are used to genetically map the diazotroph communities specifically associated with these quantified morphological structures. By statistically correlating precise host morphological shape variables with the genetic makeup and nif gene abundance of the microbial consortia, the research can test hypotheses on how host morphology filters, selects, or co-evolves with functionally critical microbial partners. This synergy moves beyond parallel characterization to uncover form-function-evolution linkages across symbiotic systems.

ideas.title: Experimental quantification of the keystone functions and trophic impact of Betapista dekkerae within its benthic community
ideas.description: This redesigned study implements a manipulative field experiment to test the causal role of Betapista dekkerae by comparing control plots with plots from which the organism has been excluded. Concurrently, stable isotope analysis of carbon and nitrogen will be used to rigorously quantify its trophic position and dietary sources, while eDNA metabarcoding of gut contents and surrounding sediment will detail its prey spectrum and associated microbiome. Finally, reciprocal transplant experiments and monitoring of biogeochemical fluxes (e.g., nutrient release, sediment stability) in exclusion vs. control plots will directly measure the organism's functional impact on key ecosystem processes.

ideas.title: Phylogenetically-informed isotope tracing of N-P coupling in Terebellidae bioturbation
ideas.description: This research integrates molecular phylogenetics with dual-isotope biogeochemistry to investigate how evolutionary history shapes ecosystem function. It constructs a robust molecular phylogeny of Terebellidae polychaetes and, for key clades, measures the δ¹⁵N of nitrate and δ¹⁸O of phosphate in their burrow microenvironments. This approach tests whether phylogenetic divergence correlates with distinct N-P coupling signatures, revealing if evolutionary innovations in behavior or physiology drive specific nutrient cycling pathways. The synergy lies in using phylogeny as a predictive framework to explain functional biogeochemical diversity, moving beyond correlation to an evolutionary mechanism for sediment nutrient dynamics.

ideas.title: Applying Microbial Keystone Species Network Analysis to Betapista dekkerae
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from microbial ecology to investigate the ecological role of the newly described genus Betapista, specifically B. dekkerae. In microbial studies, the function of a novel bacterium is often inferred by analyzing its co-occurrence network with other community members across many samples. We propose applying this analogous method: conducting systematic, replicated field surveys of the benthic habitat to document the precise abundance and distribution of B. dekkerae alongside all other observable macro-organisms. Using statistical co-occurrence network analysis, we will identify significant positive and negative associations between B. dekkerae and other species. This pattern-based approach will allow us to generate testable hypotheses about its ecological role—such as whether it acts as a keystone species, a competitor for space or resources, or is linked to specific prey or predators—effectively mapping its place in the community web to guide subsequent focused research on its feeding and direct interactions.

ideas.title: Inverse morphometric constraint on microbial process rates using biogenic mineral signatures
ideas.description: This research direction applies the high-dimensional shape analysis of geometric morphometrics—typically used for taxonomic classification of structures like chaetae—to the physical morphology of biogenic mineral particles that form in the water column or sediment. The key innovation is to treat the particle's shape as a complex, multivariate signature that encodes information about the dynamic chemical environment during its formation, including isotopic fractionation processes. By fusing this with inverse modeling techniques from biogeochemistry, one can use populations of particles with measured δ¹⁵N (or other isotopes) and quantified shapes to inversely estimate the rates and spatial patterns of processes like denitrification, which are otherwise difficult to constrain from bulk measurements alone. This provides a spatially and temporally resolved paleo-proxy by linking process kinetics directly to morphological variation.

ideas.title: Quantifying Local Nitrogen Recycling Timescales via Synchronized Dye and Isotope Tracer Release
ideas.description: This research program would conduct a coordinated dual-tracer release experiment in a region with a documented low-δ¹⁵N subsurface signal. A conservative dye tracer would be injected to directly quantify the physical dilution and transport timescale of the water mass hypothesized to host locally recycled nitrogen. Concurrently, a pulse of isotopically labeled nitrogen (e.g., ¹⁵N-nitrate) would be introduced to the same patch, tracking its uptake, remineralization, and isotopic evolution within the physically defined dye-tagged parcel. By comparing the rates of dye dispersion and the development of the low-δ¹⁵N signature in particulate and dissolved pools, the experiment can determine if the isotopic signal forms on timescales faster than physical export, providing definitive evidence for in situ recycling as the source.

ideas.title: Applying Turbulent Mixing Models from Engineering to Oceanic Nitrate Exchange
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution strategies from chemical engineering and turbulent reactor design to study mesoscale eddy effects on nitrate isotopes. In engineering, the impact of turbulence on mixing and reactions is studied using scaled-down physical models and CFD to isolate and parameterize eddy-driven processes. The adapted research idea applies this by using a laboratory-scale rotating tank to simulate oceanic mesoscale eddies under controlled conditions. Two water masses with isotopically distinct nitrate tracers (e.g., different δ¹⁵N) are introduced, and a reproducible eddy field is generated. The lateral exchange and mixing of the nitrate isotopes are then precisely quantified as a function of fundamental eddy parameters (like Rossby and Reynolds numbers). This scaled-down, mechanistic approach aims to derive parameterizations for the sub-grid-scale effects of eddies on nutrient transport, directly informing and improving larger-scale biogeochemical ocean models.

ideas.title: Calibrating Lagrangian Isotope Pathways with Controlled Dye Release Experiments
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a coordinated observational and numerical modeling campaign where deliberate dye tracer releases are conducted concurrently with high-resolution, three-dimensional Lagrangian particle tracking simulations in the same ocean region. The direct, empirical transport timescales and dispersion rates measured by the dye provide a rigorous calibration for the numerical particle models, significantly reducing their uncertainty. The calibrated model is then used to trace the origins and pathways of water masses with distinct isotopic (δ¹⁵N) signatures, enabling a mechanistic and quantitatively robust diagnosis of the physical transport timescales required for isotope homogenization, a link that neither approach could establish definitively on its own.

ideas.title: Test the role of fine-scale oceanographic features as disruptors of equatorial Pacific nitrate δ¹⁵N homogeneity
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of rapid zonal homogenization of nitrate δ¹⁵N in the equatorial Pacific by drawing an analogy from atmospheric science. In that field, assumptions about uniform atmospheric composition were overturned by identifying fine-scale features (e.g., convective cells, jets) that create and maintain chemical heterogeneity. The analogous solution involved targeted, high-resolution sampling combined with Lagrangian modeling to trace the history of air parcels. Adapted to oceanography, the research would employ high-resolution spatial sampling (via AUVs or dense ship transects) of nitrate δ¹⁵N across features like tropical instability waves and eddies. Concurrently, Lagrangian particle tracking models would simulate the pathways of water parcels to test whether these fine-scale features act as persistent disruptors, creating and maintaining isotopic heterogeneity against the assumed background of zonal uniformity.

ideas.title: Integrative Niche Modeling to Guide and Validate the Formal Description of Cryptic Polychaete Species
ideas.description: This research fuses formal taxonomy with predictive ecology by first using the genetic and morphological data from the characterized but unnamed Pista and Polycirrus species to build preliminary ecological niche models (ENMs). These models predict the potential geographic ranges and environmental niches of these cryptic species, identifying key areas where they are likely to occur but remain unrecorded. Targeted field sampling is then conducted in these predicted 'distribution gap' areas to collect additional specimens, which are used to refine the morphological diagnoses and strengthen the formal descriptions. This integrated approach not only accelerates the discovery and description process but also embeds an initial hypothesis about the species' ecology and biogeography directly into its taxonomic foundation.

ideas.title: Dynamic Niche Modeling for Terebellids Using Biogeochemical Trait-Based Dispersal Kernels
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that geographic ranges for terebellids are static and well-known by adapting a solution from phytoplankton biogeography. In that field, dynamic models couple species' functional traits with 3D ocean circulation and biogeochemical models to simulate dispersal and project shifting ranges under environmental change. The adapted research idea applies this framework to terebellid polychaetes by integrating their larval dispersal traits (e.g., pelagic larval duration) and adult physiological tolerances with high-resolution hydrodynamic and benthic habitat models. This approach would simulate larval transport and habitat suitability under various climate scenarios, generating probabilistic, time-varying range shift projections rather than static maps, fundamentally re-framing how we understand and predict their potential distributions.

The system employs an iterative cycle where initial Species Distribution Models (SDMs) based on available data identify areas of high model uncertainty and potential range edges. These areas are then prioritized for targeted field sampling and environmental DNA (eDNA) surveys. Collected specimens are genetically barcoded to confirm species identities and detect cryptic diversity, refining the taxonomic units for modeling. The updated, vetted occurrence data then feeds back into improved SDMs under climate change scenarios, creating a dynamic and increasingly robust projection of potential range shifts.

ideas.title: Adapting Geochemical Anomaly Detection to Predict Species Distribution Gaps
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from mineral exploration geochemistry to the problem of predicting distribution gaps for unrecorded species. In geochemistry, models detect spatial anomalies in elemental concentrations to find concealed mineral deposits. The adapted approach involves creating a model of expected species richness or community composition for a taxonomic group (e.g., beetles, ferns) across a region, based on environmental variables and the distribution of known species. Areas where the actual recorded biodiversity (or lack thereof) is a significant statistical outlier from this predicted background model are identified as 'distribution gap anomalies.' These anomalies are high-priority targets for field surveys, as the mismatch between predicted suitable habitat and observed species records may indicate the presence of unrecorded species, offering a more targeted method than standard species distribution modeling alone.

ideas.title: Applying Epidemiological Contact Tracing Networks to Map Marine Gene Flow
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from epidemiology, where contact tracing networks model pathogen spread, to investigate gene flow and population structure in widespread species like the polychaete Pista brevibranchiata. The analogous solution involves using genetic sequences from across a geographic range to reconstruct transmission pathways and identify key nodes and barriers. The adapted idea treats populations as nodes in a network and shared genetic variants as connections, applying network analysis tools from epidemiology to map dispersal corridors, pinpoint populations that act as genetic hubs or sources, and identify biogeographic barriers that restrict gene flow, thereby revealing the dynamic population structure of a marine species across its entire range.

ideas.title: Iterative DNA-validated niche modeling for cryptic range expansion detection
ideas.description: This research direction integrates ecological niche modeling (ENM) and DNA barcoding into a cyclical, iterative process. First, ENM is used to predict potential distribution gaps or range expansions for a species. Targeted field sampling is then conducted in these high-probability, under-surveyed areas. Specimens collected are definitively identified using standardized COI barcoding, which confirms new records and filters out misidentifications. The genetically verified presence/absence data is then fed back into the ENM to recalibrate and improve the model's predictions, creating a powerful feedback loop for accurately mapping and understanding dynamic species distributions.

ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from conservation biology—predictive species distribution modeling—to the problem of expanding biogeographic surveys for new terebellid records. In the analogous field, models that correlate species occurrences with environmental variables are used to create habitat suitability maps, which then guide efficient field surveys for rare or shifting species. Adapted to this context, the research would involve building such models for species like Laphania boecki and Pista brevibranchiata using their known records and oceanographic data. The resulting predictions would strategically target survey efforts to the most promising, yet unsampled, adjacent regions, thereby providing a more rigorous and hypothesis-driven test of the new range extensions and their potential environmental correlates.

ideas.title: Adapt Lagrangian Particle Tracking from Atmospheric Pollutant Source Attribution
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from atmospheric science, where backward-in-time Lagrangian particle dispersion models are used to pinpoint the source regions of atmospheric pollutants. The analogous solution involves running a high-resolution model (like FLEXPART) in reverse time from a receptor point to map probable source origins. Adapted to oceanography, the method would involve using a high-resolution ocean circulation model to run a Lagrangian particle model backward in time from locations where low-δ¹⁵N water is observed. By releasing a large ensemble of virtual particles at the measurement site and advecting them backward using archived 3D velocity fields, the technique will statistically identify the upstream geographic source regions, pathways, and potential subsurface processes responsible for generating the distinct isotopic signal, effectively solving the inverse problem of water mass origin.

By releasing dye in regions adjacent to newly documented species ranges (e.g., near Laphania boecki records), researchers can directly quantify larval dispersal pathways and connectivity timescales between populations. This allows the testing of whether observed range extensions are facilitated by specific, measurable currents and transport events. The approach transforms passive observation of species distributions into an active, process-oriented investigation of the larval supply mechanism itself.

ideas.title: Re-evaluating regional ecological networks by integrating Betapista's true morphological identity and functional role
ideas.description: This research integrates taxonomic re-evaluation with focused autecology to critically assess the historical ecological role of a key habitat-structuring organism. By applying integrated taxonomic methods to specimens historically identified as related sabellid or serpulid worms in the region, the study first determines the true historical presence and distribution of the newly described genus Betapista. It then uses this corrected faunal list to re-analyze past ecological studies, habitat maps, and interaction networks, specifically investigating how the misidentification or omission of Betapista dekkerae has skewed understanding of local biodiversity, niche partitioning, and ecosystem engineering. The result is a revised model of the region's benthic ecology that is grounded in accurate taxonomy and the empirically determined functional role of this genus.

ideas.title: Integrating geometric morphometrics of chaetae with population genomics to resolve cryptic structure and adaptive divergence in widespread terebellids
ideas.description: This research integrates geometric morphometric analysis of terebellid polychaete chaetae with population genomic data from widespread species like Pista brevibranchiata. By quantifying subtle, heritable variation in chaetal shape across a species' range, the study tests whether observed population genetic structure corresponds to adaptive morphological divergence in these key functional structures. This synergy allows for the identification of cryptic lineages not evident from traditional morphology alone and directly links specific genomic regions under selection to a quantifiable, ecologically relevant phenotypic trait. The approach bridges microevolutionary process (selection on chaetal form for local substrate or feeding) with macroevolutionary pattern (population divergence and speciation) in marine invertebrates.

These data are directly linked to phylogenetic and taxonomic databases, enabling rigorous comparative morphometrics and statistical testing of species boundaries. The output moves beyond descriptive visualization to provide actionable, data-driven characters for phylogenetic placement and species delimitation.

ideas.title: Apply Pharmacokinetic Tracer Methods to Magmatic Systems
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core methodological approach from pharmacology/pharmacokinetics to volcanology. In pharmacokinetics, inert tracers are injected into a circulatory system to directly measure transport and mixing rates via concentration time-series. The analogous solution is adapted by proposing the injection of spectroscopically distinct, inert gas tracers (like noble gas mixtures or SF6) into an active volcanic fumarole. By monitoring the arrival times and concentration decay curves of these tracers at other surface vents or in the volcanic plume with high temporal resolution, researchers can directly quantify the transport timescales, connectivity, and effective 'circulation' rates governing isotope and volatile homogenization in the magmatic system, moving beyond indirect geochemical inferences.

It combines a phylogenomic dataset from high-throughput sequencing of carefully selected taxa, including type species, with a detailed morphological matrix derived from re-examination of specimens. This integrated framework directly tests the congruence between molecular and classical systematic characters, mitigating biases from either data type alone. The resulting analysis will provide a strongly supported foundation for testing generic monophyly and proposing a stable, evolutionarily informed classification for the family.

ideas.title: Phylogenetically-Calibrated Faunal Inventory: Overcoming Cryptic Diversity with Mandatory Molecular Baselines
ideas.description: This research establishes a protocol where species presence/absence in a region is determined through a mandatory integration of DNA-based species delimitation with subsequent morphological diagnosis. The core innovation is the reversal of the traditional workflow: molecular data (initially COI barcoding, expanded as needed) is used first to identify putative species boundaries and reveal cryptic lineages within morphologically defined taxa. Morphological analysis is then rigorously focused on these delimited groups to find diagnostic characters, ensuring the final faunal list reflects actual evolutionary diversity rather than just perceived morphological variation. This approach systematically uncovers hidden diversity and creates a robust, falsifiable baseline for conservation, biogeography, and ecological study.

ideas.title: Quantifying the functional ecology of Betapista dekkerae through geometric morphometric analysis of its chaetal morphology
ideas.description: This research applies geometric morphometrics, a high-resolution shape analysis technique, to the chaetae (bristles) of the newly described deep-sea tubeworm Betapista dekkerae. By quantifying the 3D shape variation of its different chaetal types (e.g., feeding palps, tube-building uncini), the study aims to infer its specific feeding mechanisms (e.g., deposit-feeding, suspension-feeding) and tube construction strategies. This morphometric data will then be correlated with in-situ ecological observations of its habitat and tube architecture, testing hypotheses about how chaetal form directly dictates ecological function. The synergy lies in using a paleontological tool for precise morphological quantification to unlock a mechanistic understanding of the autecology of a recently discovered living genus.

ideas.title: Inverse estimation of denitrification rates via isotopic Bayesian tomography
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to inverse problems from medical imaging and seismology—specifically, tomographic reconstruction—to constrain water column denitrification. In the analogous fields, sparse measurements of a propagated signal are used to infer the internal spatial distribution of a source property via Bayesian inverse modeling. The adapted research idea treats the measured spatial distribution of δ¹⁵N-NO₃ in a water body as the observed signal. A Bayesian inverse model is then constructed, incorporating a prior for denitrification rates based on known environmental controls and a forward model simulating isotopic fractionation during denitrification. The model inversely solves for the most probable 3D field of denitrification rates that, when run through the forward model, best matches the observed isotopic data, thereby quantitatively constraining a key but difficult-to-measure process.

ideas.title: Bias-Aware Ensemble Niche Modeling for Robust Prediction of Undocumented Species Distributions
ideas.description: This approach enhances traditional species distribution modeling by directly accounting for and correcting spatial sampling bias in biodiversity databases. It integrates a model of observer accessibility to distinguish true environmental gaps from mere data gaps, using an ensemble of bias-corrected algorithms. The system then identifies high-probability areas for undocumented species occurrence by intersecting regions of high environmental suitability with low historical sampling effort. This results in more reliable and actionable predictions for targeted biodiversity discovery and conservation planning.
