ideas.title: Phylogenetically-informed prediction of terebellid morphology from habitat and evolutionary history
ideas.description: This study uses a phylogenetic comparative framework to disentangle the effects of habitat adaptation from phylogenetic inertia when predicting terebellid worm morphology. It constructs a robust phylogeny for the target taxa and employs models like phylogenetic generalized least squares (PGLS) to test if environmental variables (e.g., sediment type, flow regime) significantly predict morphological traits after accounting for evolutionary relationships. The approach quantifies the relative contribution of habitat versus phylogeny to morphological variance, significantly strengthening causal inference. This results in a more robust and evolutionarily explicit model of form-function-environment relationships.

ideas.title: Virtual Morphospace Extrapolation for Species with Limited Specimens
ideas.description: This approach creates a computational pipeline for estimating intraspecific variation when physical specimens are critically limited. It begins by generating high-fidelity 3D models of all extant type and historical specimens for a target species. A probabilistic model, trained on the correlation structures of morphological traits in better-sampled sister taxa or genera, is then used to extrapolate a likely morphospace around the measured type specimens. The output is a statistically bounded prediction of key character variation, accompanied by explicit metrics of confidence and uncertainty, thus providing a quantitative, hypothesis-driven estimate where traditional assessment is impossible.

ideas.title: Predictive modeling of morphological thresholds for species delimitation across Terebellidae
ideas.description: This study moves beyond simply detecting cryptic species to building a predictive model for when morphology accurately defines terebellid species boundaries. We will integrate high-resolution 3D morphometrics of key taxonomic characters with multi-locus genetic data (COI, 16S, 18S, 28S) across a phylogenetically and geographically broad sample of terebellid taxa. Using comparative phylogenetic methods and regression modeling, we will quantify the relationship between morphological disparity and genetic distance to identify if specific trait complexes or divergence thresholds are robust predictors of genetic species boundaries. This framework will explicitly test for and account for confounding factors like geographic distance and phylogenetic inertia, providing a generalizable tool for assessing the reliability of morphology in polychaete systematics.

ideas.title: Applying Cryptographic Hash Standards to Formalize Unnamed Polychaete Species Descriptions
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from cryptography and digital standards. In that field, entities are formally described using standardized, machine-readable formats (like JSON Web Tokens) with mandatory fields and canonical serialization to ensure unambiguous interpretation. Adapting this solution, the research would develop a formal, structured schema for taxonomic descriptions. This schema would be applied to provide formal descriptions for the unnamed Pista and Polycirrus species, mandating specific morphological, molecular, and ecological character sets in a defined order. The outcome would be a set of species descriptions that are not only formally valid but also computationally tractable, ensuring clarity, completeness, and seamless integration into future digital taxonomic frameworks and comparative analyses.

ideas.title: Integrating Morphological, Genetic, and Acoustic Data for a Multi-Modal Faunal Census
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that traditional, morphology-based faunal lists are accurate and complete. Drawing an analogy from astrophysics, where exoplanet catalogs improved vastly through multi-method confirmation protocols, this idea adapts that solution to taxonomy. The adapted research proposes to re-evaluate species presence/absence in a target region by implementing an integrated taxonomic method. Similar to how an exoplanet candidate requires validation by multiple detection techniques, a species record would require corroboration from independent data streams—morphology, genetics, and/or bioacoustics—to be considered confirmed. This multi-modal approach would create a more robust, reliable, and reproducible faunal census, correcting for misidentifications, cryptic species, and observer bias inherent in single-method surveys.

ideas.description: This system validates new geographic records using an initial COI screen, followed by an automated analytical pipeline that detects genetic conflicts indicative of cryptic diversity or introgression. For any flagged specimen, the protocol triggers sequencing of additional, pre-defined nuclear (e.g., ITS2, H3) and mitochondrial markers to clarify species boundaries. The final record confirmation requires a consensus from both the multi-locus genetic data and a standardized morphological or ecological assessment, ensuring robust identifications even in taxonomically challenging groups.

ideas.title: Integrative Phylogenomics of Terebellidae: Merging Micro-CT Morphology with Molecular Data
ideas.description: This research program constructs a comprehensive phylogeny of Terebellidae by integrating two previously disparate data types: molecular sequences and detailed internal morphology from micro-CT scans of type and critical specimens. The micro-CT data provides a quantifiable, non-destructive source of internal anatomical characters (e.g., musculature, gland systems, tube structure) from the very specimens that define taxonomic groups, which can be coded as discrete or continuous characters for phylogenetic analysis. By analyzing molecular and morphological datasets jointly in a total-evidence framework, the study can explicitly test the homology and phylogenetic signal of internal anatomical features, resolve conflicts between traditional morphology-based classifications and molecular trees, and elucidate the evolution of key internal structures. This synergy moves beyond separate analyses to a unified test of systematic hypotheses, directly linking genotype, phenotype, and taxonomic concept.

ideas.title: Apply a Standardized Digital Object Identifier (DOI) System for Genetic Vouchers in New Range Records
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from academic publishing—the standardized Digital Object Identifier (DOI) system—to the challenge of verifying species identifications across new geographic ranges using DNA barcoding. The analogous problem in publishing is ensuring permanent, reliable access to cited digital objects, solved by assigning unique, persistent DOIs. The adapted research idea proposes creating a mandatory 'Genetic Voucher Identifier' (GVI) system for new range records. Whenever a COI sequence is used to confirm a species in a new location, a standardized GVI is generated and must be cited in the publication. This GVI acts as a permanent, citable link that bundles and points unambiguously to the specific specimen data, raw sequence traces, the finalized COI barcode on BOLD, the GenBank accession, and collection metadata. This solves current issues of fragmented data, inaccessible sequences, and unverifiable claims by enforcing a unified, traceable standard for reproducibility in biogeographic research.

ideas.title: Population Sampling in Systematics: A Clinical Trial Model for Type Specimens
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a historical type specimen (the holotype) is fully representative of its species by drawing an analogy from clinical trial design. In clinical trials, drug effects are validated across a diverse, statistically powered sample of a population, not a single individual. The analogous solution is to use population sampling and statistical power analysis to ensure results are generalizable. Adapted to systematics, this means species diagnoses should be based on a pre-determined, statistically justified sample size from across the species' range, capturing intraspecific variation. Key morphological characters would be reported as population parameters (e.g., mean ± standard deviation, range, 95% confidence intervals) rather than as fixed values from one or a few specimens, making taxonomic descriptions more robust and representative of biological reality.

ideas.title: Adapting Paleontology's Virtual Preparation for Type Specimen Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from vertebrate paleontology, where micro-CT scanning is used for non-destructive 'virtual preparation' of fossils embedded in rock. The analogous problem is studying fragile, unique specimens without damaging them. In paleontology, high-resolution CT data is processed to digitally remove the surrounding rock matrix, revealing the complete fossil. This solution can be adapted to the study of biological type specimens (e.g., insects, small mollusks) that are often obscured by mounting media, glue, or storage materials. By applying the same virtual segmentation and preparation techniques to micro-CT scans of type specimens, researchers can non-destructively reveal and analyze complete internal and external morphology that is currently hidden, preserving the physical integrity of these irreplaceable vouchers while extracting novel taxonomic data.

ideas.title: Phylogenomic Analysis of Terebellidae Using Multi-Locus Data and Coalescent Methods
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from historical linguistics, where deep relationships within language families are resolved by analyzing multiple independent linguistic features with models accounting for horizontal transfer and sorting. The analogous problem in Terebellidae phylogenetics—resolving deep nodes and testing monophyly amid potential discordance—is addressed by constructing a phylogeny using sequence data from multiple, independent nuclear loci alongside mitochondrial markers. By applying multi-species coalescent models to account for incomplete lineage sorting and gene tree discordance, this approach provides a more robust, genome-scale test of generic relationships and monophyly within Terebellidae than traditional single-gene or concatenated analyses.
